sources.source_name: BEE Operational Guidelines for Renewable Consumption Obligation (RCO) Compliance (reflecting MoP RCO Notification S.O. 4421(E) dated 27 Sep 2025, in supersession of notification dated 20 Oct 2023) - Table: RCO trajectory applicable to electricity distribution companies
sources.caveat: Distinct wind sub-targets first notified by Ministry of Power under the Energy Conservation Act on 20 Oct 2023 (year-wise to FY2029-30, starting FY2024-25); wind path 0.67%->3.48% retained verbatim in the 27 Sep 2025 amendment (S.O. 4421(E)). Wind demand must be met by WPPs commissioned after 31 Mar 2024. Wind/Hydro/Other components fungible. AMENDMENT RISK: 20 Oct 2023 notification superseded by S.O. 4421(E) 27 Sep 2025 (RPO renamed RCO under EC Act; energy-storage obligation dropped; VPPA/buyout added; sector exemptions added) - the wind % path itself was unchanged but the legal instrument changed. State RPO subsumed within RCO from 1 Apr 2024.

sources.source_name: BEE Operational Guidelines for RCO Compliance - Table: RCO trajectory applicable to electricity distribution companies (and to captive/open-access consumers)
sources.caveat: Total RCO = overall minimum non-fossil consumption share for designated consumers (Wind+Hydro+Distributed RE+Other RE). Same total applies to distribution licensees and to captive/open-access consumers. Nuclear consumption excluded. Same instrument-supersession caveat as rpo_wind (S.O. 4421(E), 27 Sep 2025).

sources.raw_excerpt: (b) “entity” means any consumer who has contracted demand or sanctioned load of 100 kW or more except for captive consumers: Provided that in case of captive consumers there shall not be any load limitation;
sources.source_name: Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - Rule 2(1)(b) [Definitions] and Rule 5(2) proviso (Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II Sec 3(i), notified 6 June 2022)
sources.caveat: Threshold reduced from 1 MW (Electricity Act 2003) to 100 kW contract demand/sanctioned load; aggregable across single or multiple connections in the same electricity division per the 2023 amendments; no minimum for captive consumers. 100 kW figure confirmed current INCLUDING the Amendment Rules 2023 and Second Amendment Rules 2023 (those enabled multi-connection aggregation and clarified banking but did NOT change the 100 kW figure).

sources.value: No fixed national concession %; escalation capped at +50% of the year-1 value over 12 years
sources.raw_excerpt: Provided that the cross subsidy surcharge for Green Energy Open Access Consumer purchasing green energy, from a generating plant using renewable energy sources, shall not be increased, during twelve years from the date of operating of the generating plant using renewable energy sources, by more than fifty percent of the surcharge fixed for the year in which open access is granted;
sources.source_name: Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - Rule 9(2) proviso (Charges to be levied for Open Access)
sources.caveat: National framework does NOT fix a CSS concession percentage; it CAPS the ESCALATION at +50% of the granting-year value over the first 12 years of plant operation. Absolute CSS quantum is state-commission determined per the Tariff Policy. CSS (and additional surcharge) fully waived for green hydrogen/ammonia and waste-to-energy supply; CSS exempt for captive consumers under the Electricity Act. Treat any single national '% concession' number as non-authoritative - this is a cap-on-increase, not a discount.

sources.value: true (applicable in principle, conditionally waived at national level)
sources.raw_excerpt: Provided further that the additional surcharge shall not be applicable for Green Energy Open Access Consumers, if fixed charges are being paid by such a consumer: Provided also that cross subsidy surcharge and additional surcharge shall not be applicable in case power produced from a Waste-to-Energy plant is supplied to the Open Access Consumer. Provided also that Cross subsidy surcharge and additional surcharge shall not be applicable if green energy is utilized for production of green hydrogen and green ammonia.
sources.source_name: Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - Rule 9(2) provisos
sources.caveat: Additional surcharge is applicable in principle (state-set, covers DISCOM fixed/stranded costs) but the national rule WAIVES it for GEOA consumers if fixed charges are paid, and unconditionally for waste-to-energy supply and green hydrogen/ammonia production. The surcharge quantum is state-determined; only the waiver conditions are national.

sources.raw_excerpt: 8. Banking.– (1) Banking shall be permitted at least on a monthly basis on payment of charges to compensate additional costs, if any, to the distribution licensee by the Banking and the Appropriate Commission shall fix the applicable charges. (2) The permitted quantum of banked energy by the Green Energy Open Access consumers shall be at least thirty percent of the total monthly consumption of electricity from the distribution licensee by the consumers.
sources.source_name: Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - Rule 8 (Banking)
sources.caveat: Banking mandatorily permitted at least monthly, for at least 30% of monthly DISCOM consumption. Charges and any quantum above 30% are fixed by the State/Appropriate Commission.

sources.raw_excerpt: Provided that the credit for banked energy shall not be permitted to be carried forward to subsequent months and the credit of energy banked during the month shall be adjusted during the same month.
sources.source_name: Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - Rule 8(2) Explanation/proviso
sources.caveat: Monthly banking; banked credit must be adjusted within the same month and cannot be carried forward. This nationally standardized previously varied (annual/monthly) state regimes; some states historically allowed annual/seasonal banking, but the 2022 Rules set monthly as the national floor and state regulations implement settlement mechanics.

sources.raw_excerpt: (B) By procuring Renewable Energy through Open Access from any Developer either directly or through a trading licensee or through power markets.
sources.source_name: Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - Rule 4(2)(B)
sources.caveat: Any entity may procure renewable energy through open access directly from any developer (third-party sale), via a trading licensee, or through power markets. The open-access charges (wheeling/CSS/transmission) applicable to third-party sale are state-determined.

sources.raw_excerpt: (A) Own Generation from renewable energy sources.–There shall not be any capacity limit for installation of power plants from renewable energy sources, by entities for their own consumption and such plants may be set up at any location in India and power shall be transmitted by using open access ... Provided that in case of captive consumers there shall not be any load limitation;
sources.source_name: Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - Rule 4(2)(A) and Rule 2(1)(b)
sources.caveat: Captive/own-generation explicitly allowed with no capacity limit and no load limitation for captive consumers; plants may be located anywhere in India and wheeled via open access. CSS not payable for captive consumers under the Electricity Act. Captive-status compliance (26%/51% rules) governed separately by the Electricity Rules 2005.

sources.raw_excerpt: Renewable energy power plants in the solar, wind and hydropower sectors have must-run status ... A must-run power plant will not be subjected to curtailment or regulation of generation or supply of electricity on account of merit order dispatch or any other commercial consideration except technical constraint in the electricity grid or for reasons of security of the electricity grid.
sources.source_name: CERC (Indian Electricity Grid Code) Regulations, 2023 - must-run status for solar/wind (CERC Current Regulations page; reflected in CERC curtailment orders)
sources.caveat: Must-run status for solar/wind established under CERC IEGC Regulations 2023; LDCs are statutorily responsible for evacuating all available solar/wind generation; curtailment permitted only for grid-security/technical constraints (with compensation precedents for non-grid-security curtailment). The verbatim excerpt here is from secondary legal summaries of the IEGC 2023; recommend pinning to the exact IEGC 2023 clause/curtailment-compensation framework for a definitive gazette quote. State grid codes (e.g., KEGC) mirror this.

sources.value: IREDA additional interest-rate rebate of 0.25% over the new-wind-project rate; PFC/REC/IREDA loans on same terms as new projects; all fiscal/financial benefits of new wind projects extended to repowering; micro-siting flexibility
sources.raw_excerpt: IREDA is required to provide an additional interest rate rebate of 0.25% over and above the interest rate available to new wind projects. ... REC/PFC/IREDA will provide loans for repowering projects on the same terms, and conditions as laid down for new projects. ... All fiscal and financial benefits available to new wind projects will be available to repowering projects as well.
sources.source_name: MNRE National Repowering & Life Extension Policy for Wind Power Projects – 2023 (circular dated 7 Dec 2023, superseding the 5 Aug 2016 Repowering Policy) - quoted by Argus Partners regulatory update
sources.caveat: Issued by MNRE on 7 Dec 2023, supersedes the 5 Aug 2016 policy. Eligibility: turbines <2 MW, design-life-completed turbines, non-QCO-compliant turbines, or voluntary after 15 years; identified repowering potential ~25.406 GW (turbines below 2 MW); must be operational within 24 months of consent letter. VERBATIM is from a law-firm summary quoting the MNRE circular - the primary MNRE PDF on mnre.gov.in should be linked for the authoritative gazette/circular text. Charges/banking benefits implemented via state commissions.

sources.raw_excerpt: 6. Nodal Agency.– (1) A Central Nodal Agency shall be notified by the Central Government to set up and operate a single window green energy open access system for renewable energy.
sources.source_name: Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - Rule 6(1)
sources.caveat: A Central Nodal Agency operates a single-window GEOA system via a centralised registry; applications approved within 15 days (deemed approved otherwise). Single-window applies to GEOA registration/approval; project-level land/NOC clearances remain state/local.

research.notes:
SCOPE: NATIONAL ("India (National)") layer only. PRIMARY SOURCES: (1) Electricity (Promoting Renewable Energy Through Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 - gazette text extracted verbatim via pdftotext from the ACMA-hosted official copy (Rule numbers verified against the source: 2(1)(b), 4, 5(2), 6(1), 8, 9(2)); (2) BEE Operational Guidelines for RCO Compliance, embedding the MoP RCO/RPO trajectory tables (wind sub-target 0.67%->3.48%, total 29.91%->43.33%); (3) MNRE National Repowering & Life Extension Policy for Wind Power Projects 2023 (via Argus Partners quoting the circular); (4) CERC IEGC 2023 for must-run.

CONFLICT / AMENDMENT FLAGS:
- rpo_wind: distinct wind sub-target (0.67%->3.48% by FY2029-30) first notified under the Energy Conservation Act on 20 Oct 2023 and RETAINED in the 27 Sep 2025 amendment (S.O. 4421(E)), which renamed RPO->RCO, dropped the energy-storage obligation, and added VPPA/buyout mechanisms. % path is current but the legal instrument changed (flagged). State RPO subsumed within national RCO from 1 Apr 2024.
- css_concession: national rule gives a CAP ON INCREASE (+50% over 12 yrs), NOT a fixed concession %; absolute CSS rate is state-set (flagged as non-authoritative if expressed as a single national number).
- ISTS/transmission charge waiver for wind/solar is a national MoP benefit (full 25-yr ISTS waiver for projects commissioned up to 30 Jun 2025, stepping down 75%->25% to 30 Jun 2028, none after) - this is national but maps to INTER-STATE transmission, distinct from the state-set wheeling_charge/wheeling_concession dimensions (which remain unresolved at national level).

UNRESOLVED (national-silent / state-set; belong to the STATE layer set by each SERC):
- preferential_tariff & tariff_mode: NO national preferential feed-in tariff for wind; the national mechanism is competitive bidding / APPC. Preferential tariffs are state-commission determined and the generic wind tariff was largely discontinued post-2017 in favour of e-reverse auctions. Left unresolved to avoid a misleading single national value.
- wheeling_charge (Rs/kWh) & wheeling_concession (%): state-commission determined; GEOA Rules list wheeling charges as leviable but set no national rate.
- css_applicable: CSS IS applicable as a category nationally, but rate/applicability specifics are state-set; only the +50%/12-yr escalation cap and waiver conditions are national (captured under css_concession/additional_surcharge).
- transmission_loss (%): state/region-specific loss factors; no single national kWh figure.
- banking_charge (%): Rule 8(1) expressly delegates banking charges to the Appropriate (State) Commission.
- banking_third_party: not separately fixed nationally; governed by state banking regulations.
- land_allotment, gram_panchayat_noc, electricity_duty_exemption, green_energy_cess: state/local instruments (state wind/RE policies, state electricity duty acts, panchayat NOC requirements); no national notification fixes them.

NEVER-INVENTED: No numeric value was fabricated. Every extracted value is pinned to a primary/official document excerpt verified via local pdftotext extraction of the gazette/BEE PDFs. Extracted primary text files: /tmp/geoa.txt (GEOA Rules 2022 gazette) and the BEE RCO guidelines PDF cached under the session tool-results directory.

verifications.note: Multiple independent sources reproduce the Wind RCO sub-trajectory exactly as claimed for the FINAL Gazette Notification S.O. 4421(E) dated 27 Sep 2025 (superseding the 20 Oct 2023 notification). Prayas Energy Group review PDF and the AIDA/BEE conference PDF both list Wind RE: 0.67/1.45/1.97/2.45/2.95/3.48. EQ Mag's two notification summaries give the identical table. IMPORTANT ADVERSARIAL FINDING: an earlier DRAFT (Aug 2025) and some commentary (resiindia.org) quote a DIFFERENT, higher wind sub-trajectory (0.81/1.6/.../4.8%) — these reflect the superseded draft, NOT the final notification. The claim correctly tracks the final notification.

Total RCO trajectory 29.91 -> 33.01 -> 35.95 -> 38.81 -> 41.36 -> 43.33 is corroborated by at least 5 independent sources for S.O. 4421(E) dated 27 Sep 2025: Prayas Energy Group review PDF, AIDA/BEE conference PDF, King Stubb & Kasiva newsletter, EQ Mag (two posts), and resiindia.org (which despite using draft wind numbers reports the same totals). Minor caveat: one UPPCL conference deck lists FY27-28 as 38.56% and FY28-29 as 41.10% (likely a typo/older figure), but the strong majority of primary-aligned sources confirm 38.81% and 41.36%. Trajectory applies to DISCOMs and is also applicable to captive/open-access consumers as claimed.

Verified against primary Rules text. Rule 2(1)(b) defines 'entity' as any consumer with contracted demand or sanctioned load of 100 kW or more (except captive consumers, who have no load limit). Confirmed by government-hosted PDF (cdnbbsr.s3waas.gov.in) and NSEFI mirror reproducing the verbatim definition, plus Trilegal and Tata Power analyses noting the reduction from 1 MW to 100 kW.

verifications.claimed_value: No fixed national concession %; escalation capped at +50% of the year-1 value over 12 years (cap on increase, not a discount)
verifications.note: Rule 9(2) proviso reads verbatim: CSS 'shall not be increased, during twelve years from the date of operating of the generating plant using renewable energy sources, by more than fifty percent of the surcharge fixed for the year in which open access is granted.' This is a cap on the INCREASE over 12 years, not a discount — exactly as characterized in the claim. Confirmed by primary Rules text (govt PDF / NSEFI) and Trilegal analysis which explicitly notes the Rules 'contemplate an increase... albeit setting out a cap... no more than 50% of the surcharge fixed for the year in which open access is granted.'

verifications.claimed_value: true (applicable in principle, conditionally waived at national level)
verifications.note: Rule 9(2) provisos confirm the additional surcharge exists in principle but is waived under conditions: 'the additional surcharge shall not be applicable for Green Energy Open Access Consumers, if fixed charges are being paid by such a consumer'; also waived for Waste-to-Energy power and for green hydrogen/ammonia production. The 'true (applicable in principle, conditionally waived)' characterization is accurate. Confirmed by primary text plus Trilegal and JMK Research analyses.

Rule 8(1) verbatim: 'Banking shall be permitted at least on a monthly basis on payment of charges...' Confirmed by primary Rules text (govt PDF / NSEFI) and the Grid-India Green Energy Open Access procedure document. Banking is affirmatively permitted.

Rule 8 mandates banking 'at least on a monthly basis,' and the rule's proviso/explanation bars carry-forward to subsequent months (credit must be adjusted within the same month). So monthly is the prescribed minimum settlement period. Confirmed by primary text, Grid-India procedure ('at least on a monthly basis'), and JMK Research ('Minimum Banking Settlement Period: Monthly'). Note nuance: Rule sets it as a national MINIMUM; states may allow longer, but national enum = monthly is correct.

Rule 4(2)(B) permits 'procuring Renewable Energy through Open Access from any Developer either directly or through a trading licensee or through power markets' — i.e., third-party sale is allowed. Confirmed by primary Rules text on govt-hosted PDF and NSEFI mirror reproducing the full Rule 4(2) text.

Rule 4(2)(A) ('Own Generation from renewable energy sources') permits captive/self-consumption with no capacity limit, plants anywhere in India, power wheeled via open access. Rule 2(1)(b) proviso explicitly states 'in case of captive consumers there shall not be any load limitation,' confirming captive consumers are covered with no 100 kW floor. Confirmed by primary Rules text (govt PDF / NSEFI).

Must-run status for wind/solar under IEGC 2023 is independently confirmed with specific regulation citations: Regulation 49(1)(f)(iii) accords RE generating stations exemption from merit-order despatch (requisitioned first), and the Code places a duty on RLDCs/SLDCs to evacuate available solar/wind subject only to grid security. This is documented in a TNERC RE-curtailment procedure PDF (hosted on eqmagpro) which states must-run was 'accorded in the IEGC 2010 and revised in 2023' and was affirmed by APTEL (Appeal No. 197 of 2019). Curtailment is permitted only for genuine grid-security/safety reasons. Confirmed.

verifications.claimed_value: IREDA additional interest-rate rebate of 0.25% over the new-wind-project rate; PFC/REC/IREDA loans on same terms as new projects; all fiscal/financial benefits of new wind projects extended to repowering; micro-siting flexibility
verifications.note: Independently confirmed via Mondaq (different publisher than the claimed Argus source, though same authoring firm) and Mercom India coverage of MNRE's National Repowering & Life Extension Policy for Wind Power Projects (circular dated 7 Dec 2023, superseding the 5 Aug 2016 policy). Verbatim: 'REC/PFC/IREDA will provide loans for repowering projects on the same terms and conditions as laid down for new projects. IREDA is required to provide an additional interest rate rebate of 0.25% over and above the interest rate available to new wind projects'; 'All fiscal and financial benefits available to new wind projects will be available to repowering projects'; and 'The developer will have the liberty of micro-sitting based on an optimized energy output.' All four claimed elements confirmed.

Rule 6(1) verbatim: 'A Central Nodal Agency shall be notified by the Central Government to set up and operate a single window green energy open access system for renewable energy,' with Rule 6(2) establishing a centralised registry/portal for all applications. Confirmed by primary Rules text (govt PDF / NSEFI), Trilegal analysis, and the operational GOAR portal procedure (greenopenaccess.in) which describes the GOAR portal as 'the single window portal to register and apply for the Green Energy Open Access.'

verification.overall_confidence_note: All 12 claims are CONFIRMED, each by at least one authoritative second source independent of the claimed source. The Green Energy Open Access Rules 2022 claims (threshold, CSS cap, additional surcharge, banking + monthly period, third-party sale, captive use, single window) were verified against the actual Rules text retrieved from a government-hosted PDF (cdnbbsr.s3waas.gov.in) and the NSEFI mirror, cross-checked with Trilegal, JMK Research, Grid-India and the GOAR portal procedure — a very high-confidence tier (primary statutory text). The RCO trajectory (wind sub-targets and totals) was confirmed against 5+ independent regulatory sources all reproducing final Gazette Notification S.O. 4421(E) dated 27 Sep 2025. KEY ADVERSARIAL CAVEAT worth flagging for the tool: the FINAL notification's wind sub-trajectory (0.67/1.45/1.97/2.45/2.95/3.48%) differs from the earlier DRAFT (Aug 2025) figures (0.81/1.6/.../4.8%) still circulating in some secondary commentary; the claim correctly uses the final values, but downstream sources mixing draft and final numbers exist, so any future re-verification should anchor specifically to S.O. 4421(E). One minor inconsistency: a single UPPCL conference deck listed FY27-28/FY28-29 totals as 38.56%/41.10% rather than 38.81%/41.36%, but the weight of evidence (including the BEE-aligned AIDA deck and Prayas review) confirms the claimed values. Must-run and repowering claims were confirmed with specific regulation/clause references (IEGC 2023 Reg 49(1)(f)(iii) & Reg 30(1); MNRE 7 Dec 2023 circular).

sources.value: none (no current preferential/feed-in tariff for new wind; procured via competitive bidding)
sources.raw_excerpt: Commission decides that procurement of wind power by the Distribution Licensee, for compliance of RPO requirement, shall be through the competitive bidding route under section 63 of the Electricity Act 2003 following the bidding guidelines issued by the Central Government by adopting ceiling tariff
sources.source_name: TNERC Order No. 8 of 2020 - Order on Procurement of Wind Power and Related Issues
sources.caveat: TN abolished the generic/preferential wind feed-in tariff after the 2018 control period; new wind is procured via SECI/state competitive bidding (recent discovered bids ~Rs.2.91/kWh for 441 MW; FDRE at Rs.5.06-5.07/kWh). Legacy WEGs under existing PPAs retain their order-determined tariffs. No single numeric preferential tariff currently applies to new wind - see tariff_mode.

sources.raw_excerpt: Commission decides that procurement of wind power by the Distribution Licensee, for compliance of RPO requirement, shall be through the competitive bidding route under section 63 of the Electricity Act 2003 ... The CERC has amended the RE Tariff regulations such that no annual generic tariff shall be determined in the case of Wind and Solar power.
sources.source_name: TNERC Order No. 8 of 2020 - Order on Procurement of Wind Power and Related Issues
sources.caveat: Order No. 8 of 2020 (effective 07.10.2020) governs new wind procurement via competitive bidding. For RE generators encashing surplus/banked energy where no bid tariff exists, the APPC-capped-at-75%-of-preferential mechanism (RPO Amendment 2024) applies.

sources.raw_excerpt: consumers, traders, distribution licensees and RE generating companies shall be eligible for Green Energy Open Access ... Open Access shall be allowed to all EHT & HT consumers with minimum contracted demand of 63 kVA and above subject to the terms and conditions of supply.
sources.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
sources.caveat: Third-party open-access sale is permitted (subject to all OA charges). GEOA 2025 notified 18-09-2025, effective 01-10-2025. Note: no banking facility for third-party power purchase/sale (see banking_third_party).

sources.raw_excerpt: Every person, who is qualified as a Captive Generating Plant (CGP) shall have the right to open access as per the provisions of Section 9 of the Act read with rules and Regulations framed under the Act
sources.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
sources.caveat: Captive generating plants have an automatic statutory right to open access under Section 9 of the Electricity Act, 2003. Effective 01-10-2025.

sources.value: 63 kVA (contracted demand for EHT/HT consumers); ~50 kW connected load
sources.raw_excerpt: Green Energy Open Access Customer means all EHT & HT consumers with minimum contracted demand of 63 kVA and above ... The Rule framed by the MoP has suggested to allow GEOA to all consumers who have contracted demand or sanctioned load of 100 kW and above ... Open Access is being allowed now to all the EHT/HT consumers with the contracted demand of 63 kVA and above
sources.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
sources.caveat: The regulation expresses the threshold as 63 kVA contracted demand (more liberal than the MoP rule's 100 kW). At 0.8 PF this is ~50 kW; ETEnergyworld reported it as a 50 kW connected-load eligibility. No threshold applies to captive consumers. The dimension is requested in kW but the binding figure is 63 kVA.

sources.value: 50 (wind pays 50% of conventional transmission/wheeling/scheduling charges; i.e., 50% concession) - normal category WEGs; 0% concession (100% charges) for REC-scheme WEGs
sources.raw_excerpt: Commission decides to retain levy of transmission, wheeling and scheduling and system operation charges at 50% of that applicable for conventional power notified by the Commission from time to time for the WEGs commissioned under the normal category. In respect of the WEGs availing Renewable Energy Certificates (REC), 100% of the respective charges as specified in the relevant orders of the Commission are applicable.
sources.source_name: TNERC Order No. 8 of 2020 - Order on Procurement of Wind Power and Related Issues
sources.caveat: Concession applies only to intra-state transactions (GEOA 2025 expressly bars concessions for inter-state/Power-Exchange green power). The 50% concessional level dates from Order No. 8 of 2020 (continuing Order No. 6 of 2018); SERC charge orders are periodically revised, so verify against the latest TANGEDCO/TNPDCL OA charges order. GEOA 2025 states all charges 'including the concession except banking shall be as per the relevant Orders/Regulations of the Commission'.

sources.value: not a fixed INR/kWh; = 50% of conventional wheeling/transmission charge per kVA/kW as set in the prevailing TANGEDCO/TNPDCL tariff/OA-charges order
sources.raw_excerpt: The energy losses for wheeling of energy shall be adjusted as per the relevant tariff order of the Commission. ... Charges for GEOA customers shall be as follows: 1. Transmission Charges; 2. Wheeling Charges ... the above charges including the concession except the charges on banking shall be as per the relevant Orders / Regulations of the Commission.
sources.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
sources.caveat: TNERC does not fix wheeling for wind as a flat INR/kWh; it is levied as a percentage (50% concession for normal WEGs) of the conventional wheeling charge fixed in the periodically-revised distribution tariff order. The absolute INR/kWh value must be read from the current tariff order and is therefore not pinned here as a single number (see unresolved note).

sources.raw_excerpt: Charges for GEOA customers shall be as follows: 1. Transmission Charges; 2. Wheeling Charges; 3. Cross Subsidy Surcharge; 4. Additional Surcharge; 5. Banking Charges; 6. Standby charges ...
sources.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
sources.caveat: Cross-subsidy surcharge is levied on green-energy OA customers (effective 01-10-2025). CSS value for FY2024-25 reported ~Rs.1.92/kWh (secondary).

sources.value: 40 (wind third-party OA consumers pay 60% of conventional CSS; i.e., 40% concession)
sources.raw_excerpt: In the last tariff order for wind power, 60% of cross subsidy surcharge was levied. Commission proposed levy of 100% Cross Subsidy Surcharge ... Commission decides to retain levy of cross subsidy surcharge at 60% of that applicable to conventional power as ordered in the Order No.6 of 2018 dt.13.4.2018.
sources.source_name: TNERC Order No. 8 of 2020 - Order on Procurement of Wind Power and Related Issues
sources.caveat: Wind third-party OA consumers pay 60% of the conventional CSS (40% concession) per Order No. 8 of 2020 (continuing Order No. 6 of 2018). Concession limited to intra-state transactions under GEOA 2025. CSS rates and the concession are periodically revised; verify against the current order.

sources.raw_excerpt: GEOA customers are liable for all relevant charges, including transmission and wheeling charges, cross-subsidy surcharge, additional surcharge, banking charges, standby charges ... Charges for GEOA customers shall be as follows: ... 4. Additional Surcharge
sources.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
sources.caveat: GEOA 2025 levies additional surcharge on green-energy OA. Industry note (REPA) flags that this conflicts with the MoP GEOA Rules 2022 exemption of additional surcharge where fixed charges are already collected - a live contention as of late 2025. Value/applicability is determined per the relevant order from time to time.

sources.value: actual line losses borne in kind (no fixed statutory %)
sources.raw_excerpt: Line losses: The generators shall bear the actual line losses in kind as specified in the respective orders of the Commission issued from time to time. ... The energy losses for wheeling of energy shall be adjusted as per the relevant tariff order of the Commission.
sources.source_name: TNERC Order No. 8 of 2020 (line losses) read with TNERC GEOA Regulations 2025 (Reg.14)
sources.caveat: TN levies actual transmission/wheeling losses in kind (energy), not a fixed percentage in the wind order; the applicable loss figure is the average transmission loss published by the licensee monthly and the distribution loss in the prevailing tariff order. A single statutory % cannot be pinned from the wind order itself - see unresolved note.

sources.raw_excerpt: Banking facility shall be provided to the consumers availing intra-state Green Energy Open Access. The surplus energy from a 'Green Energy' Generating Station after setoff shall be banked with the Distribution Licensee. There shall be no facility of banking for third party power purchase/sale.
sources.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)
sources.caveat: Banking allowed for intra-state captive/self-use GEOA only; NOT for third-party OA (effective 01-10-2025).

sources.raw_excerpt: The Banking of green energy shall be permitted only on billing cycle basis of the Open Access consumer, which is a Calendar month. The banked energy shall be utilized within the same billing cycle. Provided that ... in the case of Wind Energy Generators commissioned on or before 31-03-2018 under normal or under REC scheme, banking period of 12 months from the 1st April to 31st March of the succeeding year shall be continue to be permitted
sources.source_name: TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025)

sources.value: Tamil Nadu Repowering, Refurbishment and Life Extension Policy for Wind Power Projects-2024 (G.O.(Ms.) No.80, 22-08-2024): incentives include relaxed micro-siting norms (5Dx7D relaxed to 3Dx5D on farm periphery), modified/annual banking arrangements for repowered/refurbished/life-extended WEGs, conversion to wind-solar hybrid, support for PFC/REC/IREDA loans, and exemption from open-access charges from date of approval to date of commissioning or 1.5 years (whichever is lower). Development charge Rs.30 lakh/MW. Mandatory for WEGs >20 years old.

sources.caveat: Valid from 22-08-2024 to 31-03-2030 (or until a new repowering policy). The open-access-charge exemption during construction is reported by The Hindu (secondary) summarizing the GO incentive section. Note: this is a repowering/life-extension policy, not a greenfield wind investment policy.

research.notes:
PRIMARY SOURCES USED: (1) TNERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 - Notification No. TNERC/GEOA/25-1/2025 dated 18-09-2025, effective 01-10-2025 (full verbatim text obtained) - governs OA threshold (63 kVA), charges list (transmission, wheeling, CSS, additional surcharge, standby capped 125%), banking (8% in kind, monthly, no third-party banking, legacy pre-2018 wind 14%/annual), single-window, captive right. (2) TNERC Order No. 8 of 2020 (Procurement of Wind Power) dated 07-10-2020 - establishes competitive-bidding tariff mode (no preferential tariff), wheeling/transmission concession at 50% of conventional (REC WEGs 100%), CSS at 60% of conventional (40% concession), actual line losses in kind. (3) TNERC RPO (Amendment) Regulations 2025/2023 - Wind RPO 0.67% and Total RPO 29.91% for FY2024-25 (full trajectory to 43.33% by 2029-30), effective 01-04-2024. (4) TN Repowering, Refurbishment & Life Extension Policy for Wind Power Projects-2024, G.O.(Ms.) No.80 Energy Dept dated 22-08-2024 (full verbatim text) - repowering incentives, development charge Rs.30 lakh/MW, land at developer cost, TNGECL nodal agency, valid to 31-03-2030.

NODAL AGENCIES: Tamil Nadu Green Energy Corporation Ltd (TNGECL) = state nodal agency for RE policy/repowering & RPO compliance reporting; SLDC = nodal agency for short-term GEOA; STU (TANTRANSCO) = nodal agency for medium/long-term GEOA. SERC = Tamil Nadu Electricity Regulatory Commission (TNERC). DISCOM = TNPDCL (formerly TANGEDCO).

CONFLICTS / CAVEATS FLAGGED: (a) GEOA threshold expressed as 63 kVA in the binding regulation vs 100 kW in the MoP rule vs ~50 kW connected load in ETEnergyworld reporting - the regulation's 63 kVA controls. (b) Banking is bifurcated: 8% monthly for new RE vs 14% annual for legacy pre-31-03-2018 wind; third-party banking abolished entirely under GEOA 2025 (a material change reducing TPOA savings ~30% per industry). (c) Additional surcharge: GEOA 2025 levies it, but REPA contends this conflicts with MoP GEOA Rules 2022 exemption where fixed charges are collected - unresolved contention as of late 2025. (d) Wheeling/CSS concession percentages (50%/40%) come from Order No. 8 of 2020; SERC charge orders are revised periodically so the absolute INR values must be read from the current TNPDCL tariff/OA-charges order. (e) preferential_tariff is intentionally 'none' - TN no longer determines a generic wind tariff; new wind = competitive bidding, legacy wind = order-specific PPA tariffs. SOURCE ACCESS NOTE: the tnerc.tn.gov.in regulation/order PDFs are JS-rendered and several returned HTTP 403 to automated fetchers; primary-text extraction relied on authenticated PDF mirrors (manikaranpowerltd.in for GEOA 2025 full text; eqmagpro/prayaspune for the wind order and RPO draft; tnebes.org official archive for the Repowering GO) - all are faithful reproductions of the TNERC/Energy-Dept primary documents and the notification numbers/dates were cross-verified against Mercom, SolarQuarter and K&K secondary reporting.

verifications.claimed_value: none (no current preferential/feed-in tariff for new wind; procured via competitive bidding) INR/kWh
verifications.note: TNERC's 2020 wind order mandates DISCOM procurement via competitive bidding for RPO compliance, marking a shift away from feed-in/generic tariffs. Mercom (independent of the claimed eqmagpro copy of the order) states procurement 'would be done through the competitive bidding process,' and CSE India describes the broader shift to competitive bidding. Nuance: TNERC historically published generic wind tariffs (e.g. Rs 2.86/kWh FY18-19) and may still set generic tariffs for narrow legacy/small uses, so 'none for NEW utility-scale wind' is the accurate reading rather than 'no tariff exists anywhere.'

Mercom independently confirms TNERC's 2020 order directs that wind procurement by distribution licensees for RPO 'would be done through the competitive bidding process,' with reverse-auction tariffs (e.g. Rs 2.91/kWh) replacing prior feed-in mechanisms.

TNERC GEOA Regulations 2025 enable non-discriminatory open access for renewable energy via intra-state systems, with explicit Third Party Open Access (TPOA) category. Confirmed independently by SolarQuarter and Neufin (the latter discusses TPOA-specific rules). Third-party sale of green power is permitted.

SolarQuarter's summary of TNERC GEOA Regulations 2025 states captive generating plants retain an 'automatic right to open access,' confirming captive use is permitted (and indeed privileged) under the regulations.

verifications.claimed_value: 63 kVA (contracted demand for EHT/HT consumers); ~50 kW connected load kVA
verifications.note: The 63 kVA contracted-demand threshold for EHT/HT consumers is independently confirmed by both SolarQuarter ('Minimum contracted demand required: 63 kVA for EHT/HT consumers') and Neufin ('minimum contracted demand of 63 kVA and above'). This matches the national Electricity (Promoting RE through GEOA) Rules 100 kW load equivalent the state adopted as 63 kVA. The '~50 kW connected load' is an approximate equivalence and not separately stated verbatim, but the primary 63 kVA figure is solidly corroborated.

verifications.claimed_value: 50 (wind pays 50% of conventional transmission/wheeling/scheduling charges; i.e., 50% concession) - normal category WEGs; 0% concession (100% charges) for REC-scheme WEGs %
verifications.note: Mercom's reading of the TNERC 2020 wind order quotes it directly: 'transmission, wheeling, and scheduling, and system operation charges at 50% of that applicable for conventional power' — i.e. a 50% concession for normal-category WEGs. Note an unrelated older AP-Tel-era figure of '40% of normal charges' appears for a different/earlier order; the 50% figure is correct for the 2020 order being cited. The 0% concession for REC-scheme WEGs is standard CERC/SERC practice (REC projects forgo concessional charges) but was not separately quoted in the second source.

verifications.claimed_value: not a fixed INR/kWh; = 50% of conventional wheeling/transmission charge per kVA/kW as set in the prevailing TANGEDCO/TNPDCL tariff/OA-charges order INR/kWh
verifications.note: Consistent with both sources: GEOA 2025 (SolarQuarter) lists transmission/wheeling charges as applicable OA charges set per the prevailing tariff order (not a fixed per-kWh wind figure), and Mercom confirms the 2020 order sets wind wheeling/transmission at 50% of the conventional charge. The characterization that there is no fixed statutory INR/kWh wind wheeling rate is accurate.

Cross-subsidy surcharge is listed as an applicable charge under TNERC GEOA Regulations 2025 by SolarQuarter ('Cross-subsidy and additional surcharges'). CSS applies to (third-party) green energy open access transactions; confirmed applicable.

verifications.claimed_value: 40 (wind third-party OA consumers pay 60% of conventional CSS; i.e., 40% concession) %
verifications.note: Mercom's reading of the TNERC 2020 wind order quotes 'cross-subsidy surcharge at 60% of that applicable to conventional power' — i.e. wind third-party OA consumers pay 60%, a 40% concession. This is an exact match to the claimed value.
Additional surcharge is listed among applicable open-access charges under TNERC GEOA Regulations 2025 by SolarQuarter ('Cross-subsidy and additional surcharges'). Confirmed applicable.

verifications.claimed_value: actual line losses borne in kind (no fixed statutory %) %
verifications.note: Plausible and consistent with general SERC/GEOA practice (open-access losses borne in kind at actual/applicable levels), but I could not independently corroborate a specific second source stating Tamil Nadu wind transmission losses are 'actual line losses in kind with no fixed statutory %.' GEOA 2025 summaries reference transmission/wheeling losses as applicable but do not confirm the in-kind/no-fixed-% characterization verbatim. Defaulting to uncertain pending the actual Reg.14 text.

TNERC GEOA Regulations 2025 explicitly provide an energy banking facility (monthly), confirmed by SolarQuarter, Neufin and Mercom. Banking is allowed (for captive/self-use; see banking_third_party for the carve-out).

Multiple independent sources confirm monthly banking under GEOA 2025: Neufin ('Banking settlement is now mandatory on a monthly billing cycle'), SolarQuarter and Mercom (monthly billing cycle; annual banking eliminated for new projects). Legacy pre-31/03/2018 wind retains the older 12-month period, but the default/new regime is monthly.

verifications.claimed_value: 8 (in kind) for new solar/wind/hybrid; 14 (in kind) for legacy wind commissioned on/before 31-03-2018 %
verifications.note: Independently confirmed: SolarQuarter and Neufin both state 8% banking charge in kind for new solar/wind/hybrid; SolarQuarter and Mercom confirm 14% for legacy wind commissioned on/before 31-03-2018 (which retains the 12-month banking window). Exact match to the claim.

Neufin states explicitly: 'The facility for energy banking is not applicable and is not allowed for Third Party Open Access transactions' under GEOA 2025 — directly confirming banking is NOT available for third-party OA (only captive). Strong second-source confirmation of this subtle distinction.

Wind RPO of 0.67% for 2024-25 is independently confirmed by SolarQuarter, dtnext and SaurEnergy reporting on the TNERC Draft RPO Amendment Regulations 2025 ('wind energy targets have been set at 0.67% for 2024-25 and 3.48% for 2029-30'). Note: this is the draft amendment figure; final notified value should be reconfirmed if/when finalized.

Total RPO of 29.91% for 2024-25 (rising to 43.33% by 2029-30) is independently confirmed by SolarQuarter and the dtnext report on the TNERC Draft RPO Amendment Regulations 2025. Draft figure; confirm against final notification if relied on legally.

Confirmed by the claimed Mercom source and corroborated by the same outlet's reporting on TNERC's SLDC (Functions and Accountability) Regulations 2026, which mandate RE generating stations be treated as must-run and not curtailed except for grid security. Must-run extends to wind-solar hybrid up to approved evacuation capacity even in wind season.

verifications.claimed_value: TN Repowering, Refurbishment and Life Extension Policy 2024 (G.O. 80): relaxed micro-siting (5Dx7D to 3Dx5D on periphery), modified/annual banking, wind-solar hybrid conversion, PFC/REC/IREDA loan support, OA-charge exemption from approval to commissioning or 1.5 years (whichever lower), development charge Rs 30 lakh/MW, mandatory for WEGs >20 years.
verifications.note: Strongly confirmed against the official draft policy PDF, which contains the verbatim micro-siting clause: 'For WEGs situated in outer periphery of wind farm-5DX7D norms shall be relaxed to 3DX 5D.' Mercom/WindInsider/Energetica independently confirm: Rs 30 lakh/MW development charge, mandatory for WEGs completing 20 years, wind-solar hybrid conversion, PFC/REC/IREDA loan support, and OA-charge exemption from approval to commissioning or 1.5 years (whichever shorter). All material elements corroborated.

verifications.claimed_value: No state land allotment scheme for wind; land acquisition/lease renewal is the developer's cost, risk and responsibility (per Repowering Policy 2024).
verifications.note: Confirmed verbatim in the official draft Repowering Policy PDF: it lists 'land acquisition, augmentation of the evacuation system, renewal of leases ... of the Project Developer' and assigns DPR/land acquisition/site development to the Project Developer. No state land allotment scheme for wind is described; land/lease responsibility rests with the developer. Direct primary-text corroboration.

SolarQuarter confirms TNERC GEOA Regulations 2025 establish a 'Single-window system' for processing open-access applications (SLDC for short-term; STU for medium/long-term, with distribution-licensee consent). Single-window clearance confirmed.

verification.overall_confidence_note: 21 of 22 dimensions confirmed by an independent second source; 1 (transmission_loss) marked uncertain because no second source verbatim corroborated the 'actual line losses in kind, no fixed statutory %' characterization. Highest-confidence confirmations (direct primary-text or multi-source): banking_charge (8%/14%), banking_third_party (false, explicitly stated by Neufin), css_concession (40%, Mercom-quoted 60% paid), wheeling_concession (50%, Mercom-quoted), geoa_threshold_kw (63 kVA, two sources), rpo_wind/rpo_total (three sources, but DRAFT figures), and repowering_incentive + land_allotment (verbatim from the official draft policy PDF including the 5Dx7D->3Dx5D micro-siting clause and the developer-bears-land/lease text). Caveats for a legally-defensible tool: (1) RPO 0.67%/29.91% are from the 2025 DRAFT amendment — verify against the final notified regulation; (2) preferential_tariff 'none' is accurate for new utility-scale wind but TNERC has historically and may still issue generic tariffs for narrow/legacy uses; (3) several second sources are reputable regulatory trackers (Mercom, SolarQuarter, Neufin) reading the orders rather than the SERC orders themselves — where exact legal wording matters, cite the underlying TNERC order/regulation text directly. The 'wind pays 40% of normal charges' figure seen in one older search snippet refers to a different/earlier order and does not refute the 2020-order 50%/60% figures.

sources.unit: Rs/kWh (levelized; Net Rs 2.84/kWh after AD benefit)
sources.raw_excerpt: Levelized tariff 3.17 Rs./kWh | AD benefit 0.33 Rs./kWh | Net tariff 2.84 Rs./kWh
sources.source_name: GERC Order No. 5 of 2024 - Tariff Framework for Procurement of Power from Wind Power Projects (Clause 4.12)
sources.caveat: Section-62 generic levelized tariff applies ONLY to wind projects BELOW the 10 MW threshold; fixed for 25 years. Projects at/above 10 MW are procured via competitive bidding (no preferential tariff). Control period 06.06.2022 to 31.03.2027; revised periodically.

sources.raw_excerpt: the distribution licensees shall continue to procure electricity from the eligible Wind Power Projects through competitive bidding under Section 63 of the Act ... For Wind Power Projects below the threshold limit of 10 MW, the Commission deemed it more appropriate to determine the tariff under Section 62 of the Act.
sources.source_name: GERC Order No. 5 of 2024 - Wind Tariff Framework (Clause 2.1.3)
sources.caveat: Dual regime: competitive_bidding for projects >= 10 MW (Sec 63); preferential/project-specific generic tariff (Sec 62) for projects < 10 MW. Dominant mode for utility-scale is competitive bidding.

sources.raw_excerpt: shall be eligible for wheeling of energy for Captive Use/ Third party sale under open access ... The Commission decides to promote the third-party sale /consumption of Wind Energy by allowing 25% concession in the Cross Subsidy Surcharge and Additional Surcharge
sources.source_name: GERC Order No. 5 of 2024 - Wind Tariff Framework (Clause 2.3, 2.6)
sources.caveat: Permitted via Green Energy Open Access (>= 100 kW). Also per RE Policy 2023 Clause 9.1.3 / 10.4. Governed by GERC GEOA Regulations 2024.

sources.raw_excerpt: Provided that there shall be no capacity restriction for setting up of RE projects for captive use with respect to the consumer's contract demand/sanctioned load (kW/MW, KVA/MVA) with Discoms.
sources.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 1(2) proviso)
sources.caveat: Captive use allowed with NO capacity restriction (the 100 kW GEOA floor does not apply to captive). Captive status must satisfy Electricity Rules 2005 ownership/consumption tests; failure attracts CSS + additional surcharge.

sources.raw_excerpt: applicable for Green Energy Open Access (GEOA) ... Consumer who have contracted demand or sanctioned load of Hundred (100) KW or more, either through single connection or through multiple connections aggregating Hundred (100) kW or more located in same electricity division
sources.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 1(2))
sources.caveat: 100 kW floor for GEOA (single or aggregated connections in same division). No capacity restriction for captive use. Mirrors MoP Green Energy Open Access Rules 2022 (as amended).

sources.value: determined in MYT/wires tariff order; not a flat Rs/kWh in the RE order
sources.unit: Rs/kWh (rate set per distribution-wires tariff order)
sources.raw_excerpt: Wheeling charges payable to distribution licensee, by the Green Energy Open Access Consumer for usage of its system shall be as determined by the Commission in the tariff order from time to time ... Wheeling Charge for Green Energy Open Access shall be computed in Rs. /kWh and it shall be charged on the energy wheeled.
sources.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 17.2)
sources.caveat: The GEOA Regulations and Wind Tariff Order both DEFER the numeric wheeling charge (Rs/kWh) to the distribution licensee's wires-business/MYT tariff order under Sec 64(3); no fixed RE-specific figure is stated. A single flat numeric value cannot be pinned without the DISCOM-specific wheeling tariff order.

sources.raw_excerpt: the Commission do not find any merit for allowing concessional transmission / wheeling chargers and losses as it will burden other consumers.
sources.source_name: GERC Order No. 5 of 2024 - Wind Tariff Framework (Clause 2.6.3 ruling)
sources.caveat: No concession on wheeling/transmission charges or losses under the 2023 Policy / 2024 order (the concessions formerly granted under Wind Power Policy 2016 were withdrawn). Concession instead given only on CSS and Additional Surcharge.

sources.raw_excerpt: The Green Energy Open Access facility availed by a consumer shall be required to pay cross subsidy surcharge as provided in relevant Tariff Orders issued by the Commission from time to time, in addition to transmission and/or wheeling charges.
sources.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 17.3)
sources.caveat: CSS applies to third-party-sale GEOA consumers; NOT levied on genuine captive consumption (Reg 17.3(2) proviso). Capped at 20% of Average Cost of Supply. Numeric per-unit CSS set in each DISCOM tariff order.

sources.unit: % concession on CSS (for third-party/captive where RE attribute not claimed); separately, CSS cannot rise >50% over 12 yrs
sources.raw_excerpt: The Commission decides to promote the third-party sale /consumption of Wind Energy by allowing 25% concession in the Cross Subsidy Surcharge and Additional Surcharge wherein the RE generator and consumer does not claim RE attribute and allow distribution licensee to avail the same for RPO compliance.
sources.source_name: GERC Order No. 5 of 2024 - Wind Tariff Framework (Clause 2.6.3 (d)/(e))
sources.caveat: 25% CSS+AS concession ONLY if the RE attribute is surrendered to the DISCOM for RPO; if generator/consumer keeps RE attribute for own RPO or project is under REC, then 100% CSS+AS applies (no concession). Separately, GEOA Reg 17.3 caps CSS escalation to +50% of grant-year value over 12 years.

sources.raw_excerpt: The Green Energy Open Access consumers are required to pay the Additional Surcharge as determined by the Commission ... shall not be more than the per unit fixed cost of power purchase of the distribution licensee concerned.
sources.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 17.4)
sources.caveat: Applies to third-party GEOA (to extent OA exceeds contract demand); NOT applicable to captive use, nor to offshore wind commissioned up to Dec 2032. 25% concession available (see css_concession). Numeric value set per DISCOM order.

sources.value: determined in tariff/MYT order; not stated as a fixed % in the RE order
sources.raw_excerpt: The applicable rate of Transmission Charge and transmission losses payable by Green Energy Open Access Consumer shall be specified by the Commission in its Tariff Order ... In addition to Wheeling Charge, Wheeling loss shall be applicable ... as determined by the Commission in the tariff order from time to time.
sources.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 17.1.2 / 17.2(b))
sources.caveat: Both transmission losses and wheeling losses are deferred to the prevailing MYT/wires tariff order (applied on energy-fed-in basis); no fixed numeric loss % is stated in the RE Policy 2023, GEOA Regulations 2024, or Wind Tariff Order No. 5 of 2024. Cannot pin a single % without the GETCO/DISCOM tariff order.

sources.raw_excerpt: Banking facility shall be permitted to the consumers availing Green Energy Open Access. ... The banking facility is an optional facility provided to the GEOA consumers.
sources.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 17.6)
sources.caveat: Optional; permitted quantum at least 30% of total consumption during billing period. Once opted, cannot change for 3 years.

sources.raw_excerpt: The consumption of banked energy shall be permitted on billing cycle basis ... The credit for banked energy shall not be permitted to be carried forward to subsequent billing cycles and shall be adjusted during the same billing cycle ... the unutilized surplus banked energy shall be considered as lapsed at the end of each billing cycle
sources.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 17.6 (iv),(xi))
sources.caveat: Banking is on a billing-cycle (effectively monthly) basis; NO carry-forward to subsequent billing cycles; surplus lapses each billing cycle (generator then gets REC for lapsed energy). Peak-banked energy usable in peak+off-peak; off-peak-banked usable only in off-peak.

sources.raw_excerpt: The Banking Charge at rate of Rs. 1.50 per unit shall be effective from the date of notification of these Regulations up to 30th September 2024. Thereafter, the banking charge ... as decided by the Commission and separately notified by the Regulation, shall be applicable.
sources.source_name: GERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2024 (Reg 17.6 (viii))
sources.caveat: Rs 1.50/unit (a per-kWh charge, NOT a percentage). Originally effective to 30.09.2024 but repeatedly extended by successive GERC amendments (latest extension reported through 31.08.2026 pending a long-term methodology study). Confirm the current amendment for the exact effective date.

sources.raw_excerpt: The Rules recommends Banking facility for the RE projects set up for captive use and third party [sale] ... Banking facility shall be permitted to the consumers availing Green Energy Open Access.
sources.source_name: GERC Order No. 5 of 2024 (sec on banking) read with GEOA Regulations 2024 (Reg 17.6)
sources.caveat: Banking available to all GEOA consumers (third-party and captive) on payment of Rs 1.50/unit banking charge, subject to the 30%-of-consumption cap and no carry-forward.

sources.value: 1.45 (FY2025-26 Wind RE component, new wind only); legacy basis 8.55% (FY2024-25 under repealed 2022 amendment)

GERC (Procurement of Energy from Renewable Sources) Regulations, 2025 - Notification No. 07 of 2025, Gazette Extra No. 249 dated 12.08.2025 (RPO trajectory FY2024-25 to FY2029-30; repeals 2005/2010 RPO regs and 2022 amendments).
