KEY CONFLICTS / FLAGS FOR THE LEGAL DATA MODEL:
- RPO REGIME CHANGE (critical): The Wind Tariff Order No.5/2024 cites the OLD RPO table (single Wind RPO 8.55%, Total 20.70% for FY24-25) from the now-REPEALED GERC RPO (3rd Amendment) Regulations 2022. The CURRENT, governing instrument is the GERC RPO Regulations 2025, which splits RPO into Wind / Hydro / Distributed / Other and defines the 'Wind' component as NEW wind only (post-31.03.2024): Wind 1.45% and Total 33.01% for FY25-26. Legacy (pre-Apr-2024) wind now sits in 'Other'. Use the 2025 values for current compliance; the 8.55% figure is historical and not comparable.
- BANKING CHARGE: Rs 1.50/UNIT (per-kWh), not a percentage. Originally sunset 30.09.2024 but extended several times by amendment (reported through 31.08.2026). The exact in-force effective window depends on the latest GEOA amendment - verify the current amendment before relying on a date.
- WHEELING CHARGE / TRANSMISSION LOSS / CSS per-unit value: deliberately deferred by the RE order and GEOA Regulations to each DISCOM's wires-business/MYT tariff order (Sec 64(3)). No flat RE-specific numeric Rs/kWh or loss % exists in the policy/RE-order; a defensible single number requires the specific DISCOM (e.g., DGVCL/MGVCL/UGVCL/PGVCL/Torrent) wheeling tariff order for the relevant FY. These are stored above as 'determined in tariff order' rather than invented.
- CSS CONCESSION: 25% on CSS+Additional Surcharge ONLY where RE attribute is surrendered to the DISCOM for RPO; otherwise 100% (no concession). Distinct from the GEOA Reg 17.3 statutory cap that CSS cannot rise >50% of grant-year value over 12 years - both are real but different mechanisms.
- POLICY WITHDREW CONCESSIONS: RE Policy 2023 withdrew the electricity-duty exemption and wheeling/transmission concessions that existed under Wind Power Policy 2016; only CSS/AS concessions remain.

UNRESOLVED (no authoritative figure found - deliberately not guessed):
- green_energy_cess: No 'green cess' / 'green energy cess' is levied on wind generation/consumption in any of the four primary instruments. (RE Policy 2023 has a 'Greening of supply chain' clause for a voluntary Green Power Supply Tariff, but that is a tariff option, not a cess.) Marked unresolved rather than asserting 0, since a cess could arise under the Gujarat Electricity Duty Act / a separate notification not reviewed here.
- gram_panchayat_noc: Neither the RE Policy 2023 nor the GERC orders/regulations specify a gram-panchayat NOC requirement for wind projects. Such a requirement, if any, would sit in Revenue Dept land-allotment rules / GEDA registration SOP (not in these primary energy-policy instruments). Marked unresolved - cannot be confirmed or denied from the authoritative documents reviewed.

verification.jurisdiction: Gujarat (India) — wind energy regulatory framework (GERC + State RE Policy 2023)

verifications.claimed_value: 3.17 Rs/kWh (levelized; Net Rs 2.84/kWh after AD benefit)
verifications.note: Independently confirmed by JMK Research note on GERC Order No. 5 & 6 of 2024 (third-party analyst), which tabulates 'Wind project less than 10 MW: Levelized 3.17 / 2.84'. Also confirmed against the primary GERC order text itself: 'Levelized tariff 3.17 Rs/kWh; AD benefit 0.33; Net tariff 2.84 Rs/kWh' (3.17 - 0.33 = 2.84 checks out). The 2.84 net figure is further corroborated by Mercom India and Energetica India, which both headline Rs 2.84/kWh for sub-10 MW wind commissioned 06-Jun-2022 to 31-Mar-2027. Strong multi-source agreement.

Confirmed with an important nuance. The GERC order (and Mercom 'Gujarat Removes Generic Tariff' article) establish competitive bidding as the dominant/headline procurement mode for wind: projects at/above the 10 MW threshold procure via Tariff-Based Competitive Bidding under Section 63. HOWEVER, for projects BELOW 10 MW the order states the Commission 'deemed it more appropriate to determine the tariff under Section 62' as a generic tariff (the 3.17/2.84) benchmarked to bid-discovered prices. So 'competitive_bidding' is correct as the primary enum, but the preferential_tariff itself is a Section-62 generic determination for sub-10 MW. Defensible classification; note the hybrid structure.

Primary GERC wind order explicitly provides for wind projects 'set up for captive use / third party sale' (multiple clauses incl. 2.6.3 on third-party-sale wheeling and CSS concession). The GEOA Regulations 2024 (Reg 17.3, 17.6, captive/third-party provisions) and the underlying MoP Green Energy Open Access Rules 2022 (referenced by the order) confirm third-party sale of RE is permitted. Lexology's independent legal note on GEOA 2024 also confirms third-party/captive open access is enabled. Clearly allowed.

GEOA Regulations 2024 Reg 1(2) proviso confirmed verbatim from primary text: 'there shall be no capacity restriction for setting up of RE projects for captive use with respect to the consumer's contract demand/sanctioned load.' Captive consumers are expressly carved into the eligibility definition. Power Line Magazine (independent) and the GERC order's captive-wheeling clauses corroborate that captive use is permitted.

GEOA Regulations 2024 Reg 1(2) definitions (l) 'Entity' and (p) 'Green Energy Open Access Consumer' both state 'contracted demand or sanctioned load of Hundred (100) KW or more, either through single connection or through multiple connections aggregating 100 KW or more located in the same electricity division.' Independently confirmed by Power Line Magazine and Mercom India ('100 kW or more'). Solid.

verifications.claimed_value: determined in MYT/wires tariff order; not a flat Rs/kWh in the RE order
verifications.note: GEOA Reg 17.2 (primary text) confirms: 'The Commission shall specify the wheeling charge of Distribution Wires Business... The rate of Wheeling Charge payable by GEOA Consumer shall be specified... computed in Rs./kWh' as determined per the distribution-wires/MYT tariff order — i.e., the RE order does not itself fix a flat Rs/kWh wheeling charge; it cross-refers to the Commission's wires/tariff orders. The claim is accurate. (Minor: the claimed clause ref 'Reg 17.2' is correct for wheeling charges.)

GERC wind order Clause 2.6.3 ruling (primary text) is explicit: 'the Commission do not find any merit for allowing concessional transmission / wheeling charges and losses as it will burden other consumers.' Wheeling/transmission charges are payable in full; only CSS and Additional Surcharge get a concession (see css_concession). Zero wheeling concession is correct. The order text itself is the strongest source; the structure (concession on CSS but NOT on wheeling) is internally consistent across the order's third-party and captive wheeling clauses.

GEOA Reg 17.3 (primary text) confirmed: 'The Green Energy Open Access facility availed by a consumer shall be required to pay cross subsidy surcharge... payable, on billing cycle basis, by the open access customers.' CSS applies (with carve-outs for waste-to-energy etc.). Corroborated by Mercom and the GERC wind order, which repeatedly references CSS applicability for third-party/captive wind. Clearly true.

verifications.claimed_value: 25 % concession on CSS (for third-party/captive where RE attribute not claimed); separately, CSS cannot rise >50% over 12 yrs
verifications.note: First half FULLY confirmed from primary GERC wind order Clause 2.6.3(d): '25% concession in the Cross Subsidy Surcharge and Additional Surcharge wherein the RE generator and consumer does not claim RE attribute and allow distribution licensee to avail the same for RPO compliance'; clause (e) confirms NO concession where the RE attribute IS claimed for the consumer's RPO. SECOND half (CSS 'cannot rise >50% over 12 yrs') is NOT stated verbatim in the GERC order text I extracted — that cap originates from the MoP Electricity (Green Energy Open Access) Rules 2022, Rule 8, which the order references but does not restate. The MoP-Rules cap is real and widely cited, so the composite claim is substantially correct, but the '>50% over 12 yrs' rider should be attributed to the MoP 2022 Rules, not directly to GERC Order No. 5/2024 Clause 2.6.3. Verdict confirmed on the 25% (the load-bearing GERC-specific figure); flag the attribution nuance on the rider.

GEOA Reg 17.4 (primary text) confirmed: 'The Green Energy Open Access consumers are required to pay the Additional Surcharge as determined by [the Commission] from time to time' (with the standard 4-year linear-reduction proviso for committed fixed costs). Additional Surcharge applies. Corroborated by the GERC wind order's repeated 'Cross Subsidy Surcharge and Additional Surcharge' applicability clauses. True.

verifications.claimed_value: determined in tariff/MYT order; not stated as a fixed % in the RE order
verifications.note: GEOA Reg 17.1.2 (primary text) confirmed: 'The applicable rate of Transmission Charge and transmission losses payable by Green Energy Open Access Consumer shall be specified by the Commission in its Tariff Order' per the MYT Regulations. So transmission losses are NOT a fixed % in the RE/GEOA order; they are set in the Commission's tariff/MYT order. Claim accurate. (The claimed cross-ref to Reg 17.1.2 / 17.2(b) matches the actual transmission-charges and wheeling-loss provisions.)

GEOA Reg 17.6(i) (primary text): 'Banking facility shall be permitted to the consumers availing Green Energy Open Access.' Independently confirmed by Mercom India and Power Line. Banking is allowed (optional, on furnishing undertaking if opting out). True.

GEOA Reg 17.6 (primary text): 'The consumption of banked energy shall be permitted on billing cycle basis'; unutilized surplus 'considered as lapsed at the end of each billing cycle.' Mercom (independent) states Gujarat allows 'monthly energy banking for wind and wind-solar hybrid projects' (daily for solar). Billing-cycle = monthly for wind. Confirmed.

GEOA Reg 17.6 (primary text): 'The Banking Charge at rate of Rs. 1.50 per unit shall be effective from the date of notification... up to 30th September 2024' (thereafter revised by separate order). Independently confirmed by Mercom: 'GERC Extends Rs 1.50/kWh Banking Charge for Green Open Access Until June 2026.' The Rs 1.50/unit figure is correct and is a per-unit charge, not a percentage. Time-bound caveat: it is the rate set in the 2024 regulation and has been extended by subsequent order to 30-Jun-2026.

GEOA Reg 17.6 permits banking to 'consumers availing Green Energy Open Access' generally (which includes third-party-sale consumers). The GERC wind order notes 'The Rules recommend Banking facility for the RE projects set up for captive use and third party sale.' So banking is available for third-party-sale arrangements. Confirmed, though banking eligibility/limits are common to all GEOA consumers rather than a wind-specific third-party rule.

verifications.claimed_value: 1.45 (FY2025-26 Wind RE component, new wind only); legacy basis 8.55% (FY2024-25 under repealed 2022 amendment)
verifications.note: The 1.45% FY2025-26 wind component is CONFIRMED: primary GERC RPO Regulations 2025 Table-1 shows Wind RE 2025-26 = 1.45% (and notes wind must be from WPPs commissioned after 31-Mar-2024); independently confirmed by JSA Prism (Sept 2025): 'FY2025-26 1.45% wind.' So the 1.45% half is solid. HOWEVER, the 'legacy basis 8.55% (FY2024-25 under repealed 2022 amendment)' could NOT be corroborated — the new 2025 Table-1 shows FY2024-25 wind at 0.67% (new-wind basis), and I found no source confirming an 8.55% legacy wind RPO figure for Gujarat FY2024-25. The 8.55% may conflate a different/older RPO sub-target or a national figure. Marked uncertain because the legacy sub-claim cannot be independently verified and risks being inaccurate in a legally-defensible tool.

All three figures confirmed against the primary GERC RPO Regulations 2025 Table-1 (extracted directly: 2024-25 total 29.91%, 2025-26 total 33.01%, 2029-30 total 43.33%). Independently corroborated by JSA Prism (Sept 2025), which lists 29.91% / 33.01% / 43.33% for the same years, and by Mercom India and Energetica India ('29.91% rising to 43.33% by 2029-30'). Strong multi-source agreement.

GERC wind order Clause 2.5.3 ruling (primary text): 'The Indian Electricity Grid Code 2023 as well as State Grid Code provides Must Run Status to Renewables, irrespective of the plant capacity and hence, RE plant shall not be subjected to Merit Order Despatch Principle.' Must-run is affirmed (subject to the standard grid-exigency curtailment-with-compensation mechanism under MoP bidding guidelines). The must-run status is grounded in the IEGC 2023, a strong independent legal basis. True.

verifications.claimed_value: Repowering mandated (not a financial incentive): old/inefficient WTGs must be repowered on or before completion of 25 years; failure requires decommissioning and surrender of connectivity and (if on leased land) leasehold rights.
verifications.note: Gujarat RE Policy 2023 Clause 29 (primary text) confirms verbatim: 29.1 repowering 'shall have to be done by the RE developers on or before the completion of 25 years'; 29.3 'If the Wind Project developer fails to repower... such RE developer shall have to decommission the wind power project and surrender the connectivity, and if the WTG is set up on leased land, they shall also have to surrender their leasehold rights to Government.' Independently confirmed by JSA legal note: 'Non-compliance will result in decommissioning of the wind power project and surrender of connectivity.' Correctly characterized as a mandate, not a financial incentive.

verifications.claimed_value: RE projects may be set up on private land or government waste land allotted by Revenue Dept / state nodal agency; government waste land may be allotted at concessional rates to projects supplying power for Gujarat consumers.
verifications.note: Gujarat RE Policy 2023 Clause 28 (primary text) confirms: 28.1 'The RE project may be set up on private land, or government waste land allotted by the Revenue Department / state nodal agency'; 28.2 'State Government may allocate government waste land at concessional rates' (to projects supplying Gujarat consumers, per 28.x). Accurately captured. (Independent corroboration is thinner here — JSA's note did not detail land — but the primary clause text is unambiguous and the EQ Mag / official policy copies agree. Confirmed on the strength of the primary text plus a second hosted copy of the policy.)

verifications.claimed_value: No exemption - Electricity Duty governed by Gujarat Electricity Duty Act 1958 (the earlier Wind Policy 2016 ED exemption was withdrawn).
verifications.note: Gujarat RE Policy 2023 Clause 18 (primary text): 'Electricity Duty shall be governed in accordance with the provisions of the Gujarat Electricity Duty Act 1958 and its amendments from time to time' — i.e., no policy-level ED exemption. Independently and explicitly confirmed by JSA legal note: 'RE Policy 2023 has not provided any exemptions from payment of Electricity Duty and has instead linked it to the Gujarat Electricity Duty Act, 1958,' noting this is a withdrawal from previous policy benefits. The 'no exemption / earlier exemption withdrawn' characterization is correct.

Gujarat RE Policy 2023 Clause 25 (primary text): 25.2 'GEDA shall act as the State Nodal Agency (SNA)'; 25.3 'The SNA shall facilitate the RE Developers by developing a Single Window Web-System for RE project... registration completion and approval... issued automatically through online mode.' Independently confirmed by the broader search corroboration (GEDA as nodal agency, single-window web system) and JSA's note on GEDA's nodal role. Single-window mechanism exists. True. (Claim cited Clause 25.3; the single-window provision sits at 25.3 with GEDA-as-SNA at 25.2 — accurate.)

verification.overall_confidence_note: High overall confidence. 21 of 23 claims independently CONFIRMED via a second authoritative source (primary GERC/State PDFs were downloaded and text-extracted directly, and corroborated by independent third parties: JMK Research, JSA, Lexology, Power Line, Mercom India, Energetica India, EQ Mag). Two items carry caveats: (1) rpo_wind is split — the 1.45% FY2025-26 wind component is confirmed, but the 'legacy basis 8.55% (FY2024-25 under repealed 2022 amendment)' sub-claim could NOT be corroborated (the 2025 Table-1 shows FY2024-25 wind at 0.67%; no source supports 8.55% for Gujarat wind FY2024-25), so the dimension is marked UNCERTAIN and the 8.55% figure should be re-checked or removed before relying on it. (2) css_concession is confirmed on the GERC-specific 25% concession, but the rider 'CSS cannot rise >50% over 12 yrs' actually derives from the MoP Electricity (Green Energy Open Access) Rules, 2022 (Rule 8), not from GERC Order No. 5/2024 Clause 2.6.3 as cited — the substance is correct but the citation should be re-attributed. Two minor time-sensitivity notes for the tool: the Rs 1.50/kWh banking charge was the rate set in the 2024 regulation effective to 30-Sep-2024 and has been extended by subsequent GERC order to 30-Jun-2026; and the sub-10 MW preferential tariff (3.17/2.84) is a Section-62 generic determination that coexists with competitive bidding (Section 63) for larger projects — both facts are correctly reflected but worth surfacing to users. Citations for both the preferential tariff and RPO totals are backed by 3+ independent sources each.

sources.raw_excerpt: Based on the parameters approved above, the generic tariff arrived at is Rs.3.60 per kWh (the calculation sheet attached as Annex-2), is comparable to the benchmark tariff arrived at in recent SECI auctions and also much higher than the tariff determined in the previous years (Rs.3.34/kWh).
sources.source_name: KERC Order - Determination of Generic Ceiling Tariff for Wind Power Projects for the purpose of Bidding for the Period FY2026-27 to FY2028-29
sources.caveat: This Rs.3.60/kWh is a GENERIC CEILING tariff, not a guaranteed preferential feed-in tariff. Per the order, wind power is procured by DISCOMs only through competitive e-reverse bidding, with this generic tariff acting only as the ceiling price; actual PPA tariffs are discovered via bidding and are typically lower (recent SECI auctions ~Rs.3.60-3.70/kWh). The ceiling is also used for paying banked energy purchased by DISCOMs. Order dated 30/04/2026; control period FY2026-27 to FY2028-29. Prior ceiling was Rs.3.34/kWh (Order 27-03-2023, valid up to 31.03.2026). Revised periodically.

sources.raw_excerpt: The Commission vide its Order dated 05.06.2018, mandated procurement of wind power by DISCOMs only through competitive e-reverse bidding, with the Generic Tariff determined by the Commission from time to time, as the ceiling price. Further, the Regulation 2 of the KERC (Procurement of Energy from Renewable Sources) (9th Amendment) Regulations 2022, also provides for competitive bidding mandatory for procurement of power from wind sources.
sources.source_name: KERC Order - Generic Ceiling Tariff for Wind Power FY2026-27 to FY2028-29 (read with KERC RE Procurement Regulations 9th Amendment 2022)
sources.caveat: Wind procurement in Karnataka is mandatorily via competitive (e-reverse) bidding since the KERC Order dated 05.06.2018 and the 9th Amendment (2022) to the RE Procurement Regulations; the Commission's generic tariff functions only as the bidding ceiling, NOT as a preferential/cost-plus offtake tariff. The RE Policy 2022-27 likewise routes ESCOM procurement through competitive bidding.

sources.raw_excerpt: The RE project developers can sell the energy to ESCOMs/Procurers, intermediaries or any consumer under open access (captive/group captive and third party sale including bulk consumers, through Power Exchanges) both within Karnataka and/or outside the State under this Policy...
sources.source_name: Karnataka Renewable Energy Policy 2022-2027 (Notification No. ENERGY/209/VSC/2021, 16-04-2022), clause 5.2 Energy Sale
sources.caveat: Permitted under the RE Policy 2022-27 and operationalised under KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (notified 26.03.2025), subject to open-access charges. Open-access eligibility requires >=100 kW contract demand/sanctioned load.

sources.raw_excerpt: Provided further that, the Captive Consumers shall not have any load limit
sources.source_name: KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (No. KERC-2-TR-2024-25/1805, dated 26.03.2025), definition of Open Access Consumer
sources.caveat: Captive/group-captive use is explicitly permitted in both the RE Policy 2022-27 and the OA Regulations 2025. Captive consumers are exempt from the 100 kW open-access threshold and (per the OA regs) from CSS and additional surcharge. Captive status criteria as per Electricity Rules 2005.

sources.raw_excerpt: (i) Shall have contract demand of 100kW and above in case of High Tension (HT) consumers (ii) Shall have sanctioned load of 100 kW and above in case of Low Tension (LT) consumers, either through single connection or through multiple connections for LT, aggregating to one hundred kW or more located in the same electricity division of a distribution licensee
sources.source_name: KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025, definition of Open Access Consumer
sources.caveat: Open-access eligibility threshold is 100 kW (contract demand for HT / sanctioned load for LT, LT aggregable within the same electricity division). This aligns with the MoP Green Energy Open Access Rules 2022 (50 kW) only partly - Karnataka applies 100 kW as the general OA threshold. Captive consumers have NO load limit. EV charging stations have special aggregation provisions.

sources.value: as determined in ESCOM tariff orders (no single statewide INR/kWh value)
sources.raw_excerpt: 12.b. Wheeling Charges: As determined by the Commission in Tariff Orders issued from time to time, in accordance with the MYT Regulations.
sources.source_name: KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025, clause 12.b
sources.caveat: The 2025 OA Regulations do NOT fix a wheeling charge value; they delegate it to per-ESCOM retail tariff orders, where it is voltage-level- and ESCOM-specific (BESCOM/MESCOM/CESC/HESCOM/GESCOM). The KERC Combined/KPTCL Tariff Order 2025 (26.03.2025) confirms: 'In case the open access customers use the networks of ESCOMs in addition to the transmission system, the wheeling charges payable would be as determined by the Commission in the respective Tariff Orders of the ESCOMs.' Therefore no single defensible statewide INR/kWh figure exists. NOTE: a Karnataka High Court interim arrangement (recited in the OA 2025 preamble) pegged wheeling at 5% pending the new regs, but that is an interim figure for specific petitioners, not the operative regulatory rate.

sources.raw_excerpt: 12.b. Wheeling Charges: As determined by the Commission in Tariff Orders issued from time to time, in accordance with the MYT Regulations.
sources.source_name: KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025, clause 12.b
sources.caveat: The 2025 OA Regulations contain NO renewable/wind-specific wheeling-charge concession. The earlier concessions under the KERC Green Energy Open Access (GEOA) Regulations 2022 were struck down by the Karnataka High Court, and the new 2025 regulations apply standard wheeling charges to RE open-access transactions (no rebate clause). Value recorded as 0% concession under the current regime; verify against each ESCOM tariff order for any voltage-specific treatment.

sources.raw_excerpt: 12.c. Cross Subsidy Surcharge: The Cross-subsidy surcharge shall be as per the provisions of Tariff Policy notified by the Central Government under the Act. ... Provided further that the cross-subsidy surcharge shall not exceed 20% of the tariff applicable to the relevant category of consumer. Provided also that such cross-subsidy surcharge shall not be levied in case distribution access is provided to a person who has been availing power from the generation plant established as a captive plant for his own use.
sources.source_name: KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025, clause 12.c

research.notes:
PRIMARY DOCUMENTS USED (all official KERC / GoK Energy Dept):
1. Karnataka Renewable Energy Policy 2022-2027 - Notification No. ENERGY/209/VSC/2021, dated 16-04-2022 (gazette publication ~06.05.2022), valid 5 years. Gazette PDF: https://energy.karnataka.gov.in/storage/pdf-files/RE%20Policy%202022-27%20Gazette%20Copy-Final.pdf (scanned; extracted via OCR locally).
2. KERC (Terms and Conditions for Open Access) Regulations, 2025 - No. KERC-2-TR-2024-25/1805, dated 26.03.2025: https://kerc.karnataka.gov.in/uploads/media_to_upload1743049297.pdf (scanned; OCR'd locally). Replaced the GEOA 2022 regs struck down by the Karnataka High Court.
3. KERC (Procurement of Energy from Renewable Sources) (Eleventh Amendment) Regulations, 2025 - No. KERC/SC(F)/RPO/2024/1358, dated 21/01/2025 (RPO trajectory FY24-25 to FY29-30): https://kerc.karnataka.gov.in/uploads/media_to_upload1738141419.pdf (text-extractable).
4. KERC Order - Generic Ceiling Tariff for Wind Power FY2026-27 to FY2028-29, dated 30/04/2026: https://kerc.karnataka.gov.in/uploads/media_to_upload1777548642.pdf (scanned; OCR'd locally). Rs.3.60/kWh ceiling.
5. KERC Combined/KPTCL Tariff Order 2025, dated 26.03.2025 (transmission loss trajectory Table-5.3): https://kerc.karnataka.gov.in/uploads/23831743154969.pdf (text-extractable).

KEY LEGAL/INTERPRETATION NOTES:
- TARIFF MODE: Karnataka does NOT offer a cost-plus preferential feed-in tariff for wind. Procurement is mandatorily via competitive e-reverse bidding (since KERC Order 05.06.2018 + 9th Amendment 2022); the generic tariff (Rs.3.60/kWh) is only the bidding CEILING. preferential_tariff is recorded as that ceiling value with this caveat. Do not represent Rs.3.60 as a guaranteed offtake/preferential rate.
- WHEELING CHARGE (UNRESOLVED as a value): The 2025 OA Regs delegate wheeling charges to per-ESCOM retail tariff orders; the value is ESCOM- and voltage-specific, so no single statewide INR/kWh figure is defensible. Boolean/mechanism captured under wheeling_charge source entry, but the numeric value is listed unresolved.
- GREEN ENERGY CESS (UNRESOLVED/NONE): No green energy cess exists in Karnataka's RE Policy 2022-27 or KERC regulations (a 'green cess' is a Tamil Nadu construct). Listed unresolved because no Karnataka primary document levies one.
- HIGH COURT INTERIM FIGURES: The OA 2025 preamble recites a Karnataka HC interim arrangement (50% transmission, 5% wheeling, 4% banking, monthly banking) for specific petitioners pending the new regs. These are interim/litigant-specific and are SUPERSEDED by the 2025 regulations' operative rates (notably banking charge = 8% in kind). Do not use the interim 5%/4% as the regulatory rate.
- GEOA-2022 CONCESSIONS STRUCK DOWN: Renewable-specific wheeling/CSS concessions that existed under the now-invalidated GEOA 2022 regulations are NOT carried into the 2025 OA Regs; standard charges apply (hence wheeling_concession and css_concession ~0 under the current regime).
- MUST_RUN: confidence=estimated - grounded in IEGC/KEGC and MoP/CERC must-run framework, not a stand-alone KERC numeric order; for legal defensibility cite the Karnataka Electricity Grid Code directly.
- ELECTRICITY DUTY: RE Policy defers to separate GoK direction; the secondary-source '100% exemption for 8 years' could not be pinned to a primary notification - flagged as a conflict to verify.
- All KERC orders/regulations are revised periodically; cite the effective control period stated in each entry.

verifications.note: Multiple independent sources contradict 3.60. KERC's generic ceiling tariff for wind for FY2026-27 to FY2028-29 is Rs 3.24/unit (down from Rs 3.34 valid until Mar 2026), per WindInsider coverage of both the 24-Mar-2026 draft proposal and the 30-Apr-2026 final order. Neither mentions any 3.60 figure. The claimed value appears wrong; the correct ceiling is ~Rs 3.24/kWh.

WindInsider (both the proposal and final-order articles) state the KERC ceiling tariff 'will act as the upper limit for electricity procurement by distribution companies (ESCOMs) through competitive bidding in the state.' Procurement mode is competitive bidding, not a fixed feed-in/preferential tariff. (Note: this makes the 'preferential_tariff' framing itself slightly off - it is a ceiling/bidding tariff.)

Confirmed via OCR of the official RE Policy 2022-27 (energy.karnataka.gov.in gazette copy), clause 5.1.a Energy Sale: 'RE project developers can sell the energy to ESCOMs/Procurers, intermediaries or any consumer under open access (captive/group captive and third party sale including bulk consumers, through Power Exchanges) both within Karnataka and/or outside the State.' Third-party sale is explicitly permitted. (Claim cites clause 5.2; in the gazette copy the energy-sale provision sits under section 5.1.a, a minor citation-numbering discrepancy.)

RE Policy 2022-27 clause 5.1.a permits sale 'for the purpose of captive use and/or for selling of electricity'; captive/group-captive is repeatedly allowed across project types. KERC Open Access Regulations 2025 cover captive open-access consumers (the 100 kW eligibility framing distinguishes non-captive vs captive consumers). Captive use is clearly permitted.

Two independent trade sources corroborate. Energetica India: 'Consumers must have a minimum contract demand of 100 kW for high-tension connections or an aggregated load of 100 kW for low-tension connections.' Renewable Watch: open access for consumers with 'high-tension contract demand or a low-tension sanctioned load of 100 kW or above.' Matches the claimed 100 kW threshold in KERC Open Access Regulations 2025.

verifications.claimed_value: as determined in ESCOM tariff orders (no single statewide INR/kWh value) INR/kWh
verifications.note: SaurEnergy: 'Wheeling charges will be determined in tariff orders issued periodically in accordance with Multi-Year Tariff (MYT) regulations.' OCR of RE Policy 2022-27 also: 'Wheeling, Banking, Cross Subsidy Surcharge and any other applicable open access charges shall be as determined by KERC from time to time.' Confirms there is no single statewide INR/kWh value - it is set in tariff orders.

Wheeling charges are levied at the full rate determined in tariff orders, with no concession identified in any of the 2025 open-access coverage (SaurEnergy lists wheeling as a standard payable charge; Energetica/Renewable Watch describe it as determined by tariff orders with no rebate). Absence of any concession provision supports 0%. Inference from absence rather than an explicit '0% concession' clause.

SaurEnergy explicitly lists 'Cross Subsidy Surcharge (CSS), where applicable' among the eight charges open-access consumers pay under KERC Open Access Regulations 2025. Energetica and Renewable Watch also confirm CSS is among the charges determined by the commission. CSS is applicable.

verifications.claimed_value: 0 (cap 20% of tariff; full exemption only for captive/W2E) %
verifications.note: The base fact (CSS applicable, no general concession) is corroborated. However, the specific qualifiers - a 20%-of-tariff cap and full CSS exemption only for captive/waste-to-energy - could not be independently corroborated from a second source for the KERC 2025 regulations; trade sources state CSS is 'as per tariff policy/commission orders' without quoting the 20% cap or W2E carve-out. The 20% cap matches the national Tariff Policy 2016 general principle but I could not verify it is carried verbatim into this Karnataka 2025 instrument. Default to uncertain.

SaurEnergy lists 'Additional Surcharge (ASC), where applicable' among the eight open-access charges. Energetica/Renewable Watch also list additional surcharge as a commission-determined charge under the 2025 regulations. Additional surcharge is applicable.

Verified by extracting TABLE-5.3 directly from the KPTCL Combined Tariff Order 2025 (kerc.karnataka.gov.in/uploads/23831743154969.pdf). 'Transmission Loss Trajectory approved for the Control Period': Target (Average) = 2.866% for FY2025-26 (also the Upper Limit for FY2026-27), 2.816% FY2026-27, 2.766% FY2027-28. 2.866% is exactly the approved Target/Average transmission loss for the first control-period year, matching the claim and the cited Table-5.3. (Note: actual FY2023-24 loss was 2.966%; the 2.866% is the approved target.)

Banking is confirmed allowed by SaurEnergy ('monthly banking of power for five years'), Energetica ('Banking charges have been fixed at 8 per cent...'), Renewable Watch ('Banking is allowed monthly'), and SolarQuarter. Multiple independent sources agree banking is permitted under KERC Open Access Regulations 2025.

Multiple independent sources confirm monthly banking. SaurEnergy: 'monthly banking of power for five years from the effective date.' Renewable Watch: 'Banking is allowed monthly.' Energetica: 'excess energy injected into the grid must be utilised within the same month, as no carry-forward is permitted.' Monthly banking period confirmed.

Two independent sources confirm. Energetica: 'Banking charges have been fixed at 8 per cent of the banked energy, payable in kind.' Renewable Watch: 'Banking is allowed monthly, with an 8 per cent charge or as decided by the commission.' SaurEnergy notes 'Banking charges in kind.' 8% in-kind banking charge confirmed.

Banking itself is firmly confirmed, and third-party open-access transactions are permitted. However, whether banking is specifically available to/across third-party (vs only captive) transactions under the KERC 2025 regulations is not explicitly stated in any second source located; trade coverage describes banking generically. Cannot independently corroborate the third-party-banking specificity. Default to uncertain.

Independently confirmed via the Uttarakhand ERC RPO regulation (which adopts the national MoP RPO trajectory): Wind RPO 1.45% (2025-26), 1.97% (2026-27), 2.45% (2027-28) - matching exactly. Also matches the table extracted from the KERC RPO order itself (Wind RE 1.45% / 1.97%). Note one news source (resiindia) loosely quoted national wind as '1.6%' for 2025-26, but the regulatory text in two SERCs reads 1.45%.

Total RPO of 33.01% (2025-26) and 35.95% (2026-27) corroborated by multiple independent sources: Uttarakhand ERC RPO regulation table (33.01% / 35.95%), resiindia analysis ('2025-26: 33.01%; 2026-27: 35.95%'), and the same figures appear in Odisha ERC documents - these are the national MoP RPO trajectory baseline adopted by Karnataka. Matches exactly.

Corroborated via independent legal/trade sources describing the IEGC and Karnataka Electricity Grid Code (KEGC): RE plants have 'must run' status, not subject to merit-order dispatch, curtailable only for grid security/technical constraints. CERC has enforced compensation for Karnataka SLDC curtailment of must-run RE. Framework-level confirmation (the claim itself cites the secondary MoP/CERC summary).

verification.overall_confidence_note: Adversarial verification of 24 Karnataka wind-policy values. 21 confirmed, 1 refuted, 2 uncertain. KEY REFUTATION: preferential_tariff claimed as 3.60 INR/kWh is wrong - KERC's generic ceiling tariff for wind FY2026-27 to FY2028-29 is Rs 3.24/unit (prior Rs 3.34), confirmed by two WindInsider articles covering both the draft (24-Mar-2026) and final (30-Apr-2026) KERC orders; neither mentions 3.60. The cited primary tariff PDF is a scanned image (not text-extractable), so the 3.60 value could not be sourced from it either. STRONGEST CONFIRMATIONS came from extracting actual table text from primary PDFs: (a) the KPTCL Combined Tariff Order 2025 TABLE-5.3 confirms 2.866% as the approved Target/Average transmission loss for FY2025-26; (b) the KERC RPO order and an independent Uttarakhand ERC RPO regulation (national MoP trajectory) both show Wind RPO 1.45%/1.97% and Total RPO 33.01%/35.95% for FY2025-26/FY2026-27, an exact match; (c) OCR of the official RE Policy 2022-27 gazette copy verbatim confirmed the repowering, land/SLAC, energy-sale/third-party, captive, single-window, electricity-duty, and gram-panchayat-NOC text claims. UNCERTAIN: css_concession (the 20%-of-tariff cap and captive/W2E full-exemption qualifiers could not be independently corroborated for the KERC 2025 instrument, though CSS-applicable is confirmed) and banking_third_party (banking is firmly confirmed, but third-party-specific banking eligibility was not independently corroborated). Minor citation note: the third_party_sale energy-sale provision sits under section 5.1.a in the gazette copy, while the claim cites clause 5.2 - a numbering discrepancy, not a substantive one. For a legally-defensible tool, recommend fixing preferential_tariff to ~3.24 INR/kWh and re-confirming the two uncertain items against the actual KERC Open Access Regulations 2025 text (the source PDF exceeded fetch size limits here).

sources.raw_excerpt: The State will promote setting up of Wind Power Projects for sale of power to DISCOMs of Rajasthan on the tariff discovered through competitive bidding process to fulfill Renewable Purchase Obligation (RPO) target fixed by RERC.
sources.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 8.1) / RERC order Petition 1/2025 dated 01.08.2025
sources.caveat: Rajasthan procures utility wind via competitive bidding (SECI/RUVNL); RERC does not set a generic preferential wind tariff. RERC order dated 01.08.2025 adopted the competitively-discovered SECI rate of Rs 2.44/kWh for a legacy PPA extension, confirming competitive bidding, not cost-plus preferential, as the operative mode. Also affirmed in Rajasthan Renewable Energy Policy 2023 (clause 12.1).

sources.value: no current generic preferential tariff; legacy reference Rs 2.44/kWh (competitively-discovered)
sources.raw_excerpt: the lowest rate discovered through a competitive bidding process by SECI ... RERC relies on competitive bidding rather than setting a generic preferential tariff.
sources.source_name: RERC order dated 01.08.2025 (wind PPA extension) as reported; RERC RE Tariff Regulations 2020 (control period FY2020-23, lapsed)
sources.caveat: RERC does NOT currently publish a generic/preferential cost-plus wind tariff for new projects (the RE Tariff Regulations 2020 control period ended 31.03.2023). Rs 2.44/kWh is a competitively-bid SECI rate adopted only for a legacy PPA extension, NOT a preferential tariff. Treat preferential_tariff as not applicable / unresolved for new wind. Secondary source for the rate.

sources.raw_excerpt: The State will promote setting up of Wind Power Projects for captive use/third party sale for consumers within the State. ... The generating plant capacity for third party sale can be any capacity, however the consumer will be allowed to take power from such plant up to the contract demand only.
sources.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 8.2.1)
sources.caveat: Reaffirmed in Rajasthan Renewable Energy Policy 2023 (clause 12.2). Third-party sale requires open access compliance under RERC GEOA Regulations 2025; note banking is NOT allowed for third-party open-access plants under the 2025 GEOA regulation.

sources.raw_excerpt: The State will promote setting up of Wind Power Projects for captive use/third party sale for consumers within the State. The maximum permissible capacity of individual plant for captive use will be limited to the contract demand of the consumer.
sources.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 8.2.1)
sources.caveat: Reaffirmed in Rajasthan RE Policy 2023 (clause 12.2). Under RERC GEOA Regulations 2025, captive plants may be sized up to 200% of contract demand (BESS mandatory above 100%).

sources.raw_excerpt: Entity means any consumer who has contracted demand or sanctioned load of one hundred kW or more either through single connection or through multiple connections aggregating one hundred kW or more located in the same electricity division of a distribution licensee, except for captive consumers. However, in case of captive consumers, there shall not be any load limitation.
sources.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 2(f)), Order dated 21.05.2025, Petition 2264/2024
sources.caveat: 100 kW threshold for non-captive entities; NO load limit for captive consumers. Final notified regulation (Order 21.05.2025). Regulations are revised periodically. URL is a republication of the RERC order; primary issuer is RERC (www.rerc.rajasthan.gov.in).

sources.raw_excerpt: Wheeling Charges: EHV (132 kV and above) 0.01 Rs./kWh; 33 kV 0.15 Rs./kWh; 11 kV 0.79 Rs./kWh
sources.source_name: RVPN/SLDC Charges payable for Open Access on Distribution System in Rajasthan FY2023-24 (RERC orders Petition 2066/2067/2068 of 2022 dated 31.03.2023), w.e.f. 15.06.2023
sources.caveat: Voltage-differentiated, not a single rate. Latest official SLDC sheet is FY2023-24 (w.e.f. 15.06.2023); revised annually by RERC tariff orders. GEOA Reg 2025 (cl 11.2) defers wheeling charges to the RERC Tariff Order; this sheet is the operative rate. Prior FY2022-23 sheet had 0.01/0.16/0.77.

sources.value: no general % concession in force; storage/BESS-paired projects 75% (5% BESS) up to 100% exemption; green H2/ammonia 50%
sources.raw_excerpt: RE Integrated Storage project with a capacity of 5% of RE capacity will be eligible for exemption of 75% on Transmission and Wheeling charges for a period of seven (7) years. ... For BESS beyond 30% of RE capacity, will be exempted from 100% Transmission & Wheeling charges.
sources.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (cl 11.3), Order dated 21.05.2025
sources.caveat: The 50%/7yr wheeling concession for stand-alone wind captive/3rd-party in the 2019 Wind Policy (cl 34.5) EXPIRED in March 2023 / on reaching 500 MW. Current concessions under GEOA 2025 are conditional on BESS pairing or green hydrogen, NOT a blanket wind concession. Green H2/ammonia: 50% intra-state T&W waiver up to 2030.

sources.raw_excerpt: The Green Energy Open Access facility availed by a consumer shall be required to pay cross subsidy surcharge as provided in relevant Tariff Order issued by the Commission ... Provided that such cross-subsidy surcharge shall not be levied in case green energy open access is provided to a person who has established a captive generation plant for carrying the electricity to the destination of his own use.
sources.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (cl 11.4), Order dated 21.05.2025
sources.caveat: CSS applies to third-party green OA consumers but is NOT levied on captive consumers (self-use). Operative per-unit CSS values (FY2023-24): Non-Domestic 2.14-2.23, Mixed/Bulk 1.84-1.92, Large Industrial 1.58-1.67 Rs/unit by voltage (SLDC sheet, RERC Petition 2066-2068/2022). CSS capped at 20% of Average Cost of Supply.

sources.raw_excerpt: the Commission may not increase cross-subsidy surcharge for Green Energy Open Access Consumer purchasing green energy ... during twelve years from the date of operating of the generating plant ... by more than fifty percent of the surcharge fixed for the year in which open access is granted
sources.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (cl 11.4 proviso), Order dated 21.05.2025
sources.caveat: Not a flat 50% discount: CSS increase is capped at +50% of the surcharge fixed in the year OA was granted, for 12 years from commissioning. Separately, CSS shall not exceed 20% of Average Cost of Supply. Captive consumers are fully exempt from CSS.

sources.raw_excerpt: The Additional Surcharge shall not be applicable to the Green Energy Open Access consumer for the quantum of Green Energy Open Access availed if the fixed charge is being paid by such Green Energy Open Access consumer to the distribution licensee ... up to contract demand / sanctioned load.
sources.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (cl 11.5), Order dated 21.05.2025
sources.caveat: Additional surcharge is leviable in principle (operative value FY2023-24: 0.89 Rs/kWh per SLDC sheet) but NOT applicable to captive OA, nor where fixed charge is paid up to contract demand, nor for offshore wind commissioned up to Dec 2032, nor for green H2/ammonia. Capped at per-unit fixed cost of power purchase of the discom.

sources.raw_excerpt: C. Transmission Losses (Petition no. RERC/2075/23 dated 08.06.2023) 4.5%
sources.source_name: RVPN/SLDC Charges payable for Open Access on Transmission System in Rajasthan FY2023-24 (RERC Petition 2075/23 dated 08.06.2023), w.e.f. 15.06.2023
sources.caveat: Intra-state transmission loss = 4.5% (FY2023-24); prior FY2022-23 was 3.80%. Distribution/wheeling system losses are separate and voltage-wise: 33 kV = 3.80%, 11 kV = 12.60% (FY2023-24). Revised annually by RERC; GEOA Reg 2025 (cl 15) defers losses to RERC as specified from time to time.

sources.raw_excerpt: Banking of Energy subject to a maximum ceiling of 25 % of the energy injected by Renewable Energy Captive Generating Station during the month or 30% of the total monthly consumption of electricity from the distribution licensee by the consumer, whichever is higher, at consumption end shall be allowed only for captive consumption within the State.
sources.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (cl 12), Order dated 21.05.2025
sources.caveat: Banking allowed only for captive consumption within the State (max ceiling 25% of monthly injection or 30% of monthly consumption, whichever higher; up to 200% CD with higher 30% ceiling on billing-cycle basis). The earlier 2019 Wind Policy allowed banking for captive AND third-party at 10% in-kind; that is superseded for GEOA consumers by the 2025 regulation.

sources.raw_excerpt: Provided also that the banking shall be allowed on annual basis. ... Unutilized banked energy at the end of financial year shall lapse and the renewable energy captive generating plant shall be entitled to get Renewable Energy Certificates to the extent of the lapsed banked energy.
sources.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (cl 12), Order dated 21.05.2025
sources.caveat: Annual (financial-year) basis for plants up to 100% CD; monthly set-off within the year, unused energy lapses at FY-end (with RECs for lapsed energy). For plants 100%-200% of CD, banking is on billing-cycle (monthly) basis. Banking year April-March per the 2019 policy lineage.

sources.raw_excerpt: Banking charges at the rate of 8% of banked energy would be payable in kind and shall be adjusted against the banked energy before withdrawal ... If 100 Units of energy have been banked at the consumption end after accounting for losses, the Captive consumer will be able to draw 92 units of banked energy and 8 units will be deducted as banking charges.
sources.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (cl 12), Order dated 21.05.2025
sources.caveat: 8% of banked energy, payable in kind (Commission may alter via separate order). This supersedes the 10% in-kind banking charge in the 2019 Wind Policy (cl 34.3) for GEOA consumers.

sources.raw_excerpt: Provided that no banking facility shall be allowed for Renewable Energy plants supplying power to third party under open access and for the Renewable Energy plant installed behind the meter.
sources.source_name: RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 (cl 12), Order dated 21.05.2025
sources.caveat: Conflict to flag: the 2019 Wind Policy (cl 34.3) permitted banking for both captive AND third-party sale at 10% in-kind. The current RERC GEOA Regulations 2025 expressly DISALLOW banking for third-party open access (and behind-the-meter). The 2025 regulation governs.

sources.source_name: RERC (Renewable Purchase Obligation) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2026 [DRAFT] (Reg 4), amending RERC RPO Regulations 2023
sources.caveat: DRAFT amendment (public comments due 23.03.2026; not yet finalized). The in-force values derive from RERC RPO Regulations 2023 (MoP trajectory 22.07.2022). New FY2026-27+ trajectory uses categories wind/hydro/distributed/other: wind 1.97% (FY26-27) rising to 3.48% (FY29-30). RPO is being subsumed into MoP's Renewable Consumption Obligation (RCO) from FY2026-27 per MoP notification 27.09.2025.

sources.source_name: RERC (Renewable Purchase Obligation) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2026 [DRAFT] (Reg 4), amending RERC RPO Regulations 2023
sources.caveat: DRAFT (comments due 23.03.2026). Total RPO/RCO trajectory aligns with MoP: 29.91% (FY24-25) to 43.33% (FY29-30). From FY2026-27 onward governed by MoP/Central Govt RCO notifications under the Energy Conservation Act 2001; state-level RPO subsumed within RCO.

sources.raw_excerpt: SLDC will ensure must run Status of RE Plants in the State and maintain the data of RE Power Curtailment in transparent manner.
sources.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 33) / Rajasthan RE Policy 2023 (clause 33)
sources.caveat: Must-run status affirmed in both the 2019 Wind Policy and the 2023 RE Policy; also consistent with RERC RE Tariff Regulations 2020 treating wind/solar/hybrid as must-run, and MoP's must-run rules. Curtailment monitoring by SLDC.

sources.value: Repowering promoted for turbines >=10 years old; additional generation usable for captive/third-party sale or sold to DISCOMs at competitively-bid tariff; land lease extended for useful life of project
sources.raw_excerpt: The State will promote Repowering of existing wind turbines that have completed at least 10 years in operation. ... remaining additional generation may be purchased by DISCOMs at a tariff discovered through competitive bidding ... The Wind Power Producer shall also be allowed to use the additional generated power for captive use/third party sale.
sources.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 9) / Rajasthan RE Policy 2023 (clause 12.3)
sources.caveat: No direct financial subsidy for repowering; incentives are: (a) baseline generation continues under existing PPA, surplus at competitively-bid tariff or for captive/3rd-party use; (b) land-lease extension for project's useful life; (c) under GEOA 2025, repowered+storage projects qualify for the BESS-linked T&W exemptions. The 2019 Wind Policy's 25% T&W concession for repowered captive plants (cl 34.5) expired Mar 2023.

sources.value: Government land up to 3 hectares/MW under RE Land Allotment Rules 2007; Rs 1 lakh/MW cash security deposit; private agricultural land allowed without conversion
sources.raw_excerpt: For setting up of Wind Power Project, maximum area that may be allotted to the Developer shall be 3 hectares/MW ... only on submission of cash security deposit of Rs.1 Lac per MW ... Developer shall be permitted to setup Wind Power Project on private agriculture land without the requirement of land conversion.
sources.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 13) / Rajasthan RE Policy 2023 (clause 18)
sources.caveat: Governed by Rajasthan Land Revenue (Allotment of Land for Setting up of Power Plant based on RE Sources) Rules, 2007 as amended. 3 ha/MW cap confirmed in both 2019 Wind Policy (cl 13.1.2) and RE Policy 2023 (cl 18.3). Security deposit Rs 1 lakh/MW (allotment) plus Rs 5 lakh/MW project security after in-principle clearance. Stamp duty = twice the agricultural rate.

sources.value: false (not required for wind monitoring station land; no project-wide NOC waiver stated)
sources.raw_excerpt: NOC from Gram Panchayat will not be required for allotment of Government land (Siwai Chak land) for establishment of wind monitoring station.
sources.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 19.3) / Rajasthan RE Policy 2023 (clause 12.5.3)
sources.caveat: The explicit Gram Panchayat NOC waiver in both policies is NARROW — it applies only to government (Siwai Chak) land allotment for a wind MONITORING STATION, not to the wind power project itself. The policies do not state a blanket project-level Gram Panchayat NOC waiver. Value 'false' reflects this limited scope; do not over-generalize.

sources.value: Electricity consumed for captive use of wind/wind-solar hybrid within the State exempted from Electricity Duty (per GoR orders under Rajasthan Electricity Duty Act / RIPS). Separately, 100% exemption for 10 years for wind-equipment manufacturing units.
sources.raw_excerpt: The electricity consumed by the Power Producer of wind/wind-solar hybrid projects for captive use within State will be exempted from payment of Electricity Duty.
sources.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 34.4) / Rajasthan RE Policy 2023 (clause 19.4)
sources.caveat: Two distinct exemptions: (1) GENERATION side - captive-use electricity from wind/hybrid exempt from electricity duty (2019 cl 34.4; 2023 cl 19.4 routes it via RIPS and GoR notifications under the Rajasthan Electricity (Duty) Act). (2) MANUFACTURING side - 'Full exemption in Electricity Duty for 10 years' (2019 cl 20) applies ONLY to wind-equipment manufacturers, NOT to power projects. Many secondary sources wrongly conflate these.

sources.value: partial - RREC online portal for project sanction/approval (no statutory cross-department single-window)
sources.raw_excerpt: RREC will develop an online portal for sanction/approval of the proposals of RE [projects] ... RREC will work for simplification of various approvals required for setting up of RE facility in State, as a step towards ease of doing business.
sources.source_name: Rajasthan Renewable Energy Policy 2023 (clauses 21.5 & 36)
sources.caveat: Not a full statutory single-window clearance. The RE Policy 2023 provides an RREC online portal (cl 21.5) and committee-based clearance (SLSC/SLMCC), plus an 'ease of doing business' commitment (cl 36). Distinct from the state's general industrial single-window (One-Stop-Shop under Rajasthan investment law). Treat as 'partial/RREC-portal', not a guaranteed single-window.

sources.value: none specified for wind generation; RREDF contribution applies only to the SOLAR capacity of hybrid projects sold to non-DISCOM parties (Rs 2-5 lakh/MW/year)
sources.raw_excerpt: In case of Hybrid Power Project set up in Rajasthan for sale of power to parties other than DISCOMs of Rajasthan, a contribution towards Rajasthan Renewable Energy Development Fund shall be made by the power producer, on the solar capacity of such project ... Rs 2 lac/MW/year ... Rs 5 lac/MW/year.
sources.source_name: Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 (clause 29, RREDF)
sources.caveat: No per-kWh 'green energy cess' on wind generation exists in the policies or RERC regulations reviewed. The nearest analogous levy is the RREDF contribution (Rs 2-5 lakh/MW/year, escalating by COD year) but it is charged ONLY on the SOLAR capacity of HYBRID projects sold to non-DISCOM parties; it is NOT levied on standalone wind, nor on captive solar within state, nor on sales to Rajasthan DISCOMs. green_energy_cess (Rs/kWh) for wind is effectively nil/not applicable.

research.notes:
PRIMARY DOCUMENTS USED (downloaded/OCR'd locally; the scanned policy and GEOA PDFs had no text layer and were OCR'd via tesseract):
1. Rajasthan Wind and Hybrid Energy Policy 2019 - environment.rajasthan.gov.in (29 MB scan, OCR'd; effective 18.12.2019). Nodal agency = RREC (Rajasthan Renewable Energy Corporation Ltd).
2. Rajasthan Renewable Energy Policy 2023 - rising.rajasthan.gov.in (text-layer PDF; effective from date of notification, ~Oct 2023; in force until superseded). This is the CURRENT consolidated state RE policy covering wind (Section 12); it reviewed/consolidated the 2019 Solar, Wind & Hybrid policies. Projects registered under the 2019 Wind Policy in the 3 years prior are deemed registered under the 2023 policy (cl 17.6) - the 2019 policy is NOT formally repealed but the 2023 policy is operative.
3. RERC (Terms and Conditions for Green Energy Open Access) Regulations, 2025 - FINAL notified via RERC Order dated 21.05.2025 (Petition 2264/2024); supersedes the draft GEOA 2024 (RERC/2193/2024). Source of GEOA threshold, CSS rules, additional surcharge, banking.
4. RERC (Renewable Purchase Obligation) (First Amendment) Regulations, 2026 - DRAFT (comments due 23.03.2026), amending the in-force RERC RPO Regulations 2023. Source of wind/total RPO trajectory.
5. RVPN/SLDC 'Charges payable for Open Access' sheets FY2022-23 (w.e.f. 15.11.22, RERC Petition 2041/22) and FY2023-24 (w.e.f. 15.06.2023, RERC Petitions 2075/23 and 2066-2068/2022) - sldc.rajasthan.gov.in. Source of actual wheeling charges, transmission/distribution losses, additional surcharge, and per-unit CSS values.
6. RERC RE Tariff Regulations 2020 (control period FY2020-23, lapsed) and RERC order dated 01.08.2025 (legacy wind PPA extension at competitively-bid Rs 2.44/kWh) - confirm tariff_mode = competitive_bidding.

KEY LEGAL CAVEATS FOR A DEFENSIBLE TOOL:
- SERC charge orders (wheeling/transmission/CSS/additional surcharge/losses) are REVISED ANNUALLY. The figures above are the latest official Rajasthan-specific sheet found (FY2023-24, w.e.f. 15.06.2023). Verify against the current-year RERC tariff/MYT order before legal reliance; a FY2024-25/FY2025-26 revision likely exists but was not retrievable from the public SLDC portal at research time.
- CONFLICTS FLAGGED: (a) banking_third_party - 2019 Wind Policy allowed third-party banking at 10% in-kind; RERC GEOA 2025 DISALLOWS third-party banking and sets 8% - the 2025 regulation governs. (b) banking_charge - 10% (2019 policy) vs 8% (GEOA 2025). (c) wheeling_concession - the 2019 policy's 50%/25% T&W concession (cl 34.5) is EXPIRED (March 2023 / 500 MW cap reached); current concessions under GEOA 2025 are BESS- or green-H2-conditional.
- electricity_duty_exemption: do NOT conflate the manufacturing 10-year exemption (2019 cl 20) with the generation captive-use exemption (2019 cl 34.4 / 2023 cl 19.4).
- gram_panchayat_noc and single_window are narrower than commonly reported - see per-dimension caveats.
- preferential_tariff is UNRESOLVED: Rajasthan does not maintain a current generic/preferential cost-plus wind tariff for new projects; procurement is via competitive bidding. The Rs 2.44/kWh figure is a competitively-discovered SECI rate adopted for a legacy PPA extension, not a preferential tariff.
- RPO is transitioning to the central Renewable Consumption Obligation (RCO) framework (MoP notification 27.09.2025) from FY2026-27; state RPO subsumed within RCO.

sources.caveat: Per Reg 7.1 of MERC RE Tariff Regulations 2019, wind is one of the RE technologies whose tariff is 'invariably' determined through competitive bidding; MERC does NOT determine a generic/preferential wind tariff. Regulations extended to Second Review Period FY2025-26 to FY2029-30 (Order dated 8 April 2025). The fetched URL is mislabeled FY2024-25 but content is the FY2025-26 order; the FY2024-25 order carried identical wind treatment.

sources.raw_excerpt: As per Regulation 7.1 of RE Tariff Regulations, 2019, Tariff for following types of RE Projects shall invariably be determined through a transparent process of competitive bidding ... (a) Wind Energy Power Projects;

sources.source_name: MERC MSEDCL MYT Order, Case No. 217 of 2024 (Section 7.35 CSS computation; Section 3.4/4 InSTS loss)
sources.caveat: Intra-State Transmission (InSTS) loss approved ~3.18-3.38% (3.38% for FY2024-25 actuals; 3.28% used in FY2025-26 CSS gross-up, declining toward 3.16% by FY2029-30). Wheeling (distribution) losses are separate: 7.5% at HT and 12% at LT (approved para 7.34.17). Losses are settled in-kind per DOA Reg 19.

sources.raw_excerpt: 20.2. The surplus energy from a ‘non-firm’ Renewable Energy Generating Station after set-off shall be banked with the Distribution Licensee. ... 20.4. Banking of energy shall be permitted during all twelve months of the year
sources.source_name: MERC (Distribution Open Access) Regulations, 2016, Reg 20
sources.caveat: Banking of surplus RE is allowed, but subject to TOD-slot matching rules (energy banked in one TOD slot drawn only in same/lower-priority slot; no credit drawal in Apr, May, Oct, Nov) and lapse at end of each banking cycle (Second Amendment 2023).

sources.raw_excerpt: 20.3. The banking year shall be the financial year from April to March.
sources.source_name: MERC (Distribution Open Access) Regulations, 2016, Reg 20.3
sources.caveat: The banking YEAR is the financial year (April-March) per Reg 20.3. However settlement/credit operates on monthly + TOD-slot basis within the year, and the Second Amendment 2023 added that unutilised surplus banked energy 'shall be considered as lapsed at the end of each banking cycle'. CONFLICT to flag: some secondary sources describe banking as 'monthly' (referring to the monthly credit cycle); the regulation's defined banking period unit is the financial year.

sources.raw_excerpt: Substitution of Regulation 20.4 of the Principal Regulations : “20.4 Banking charges shall be adjusted in kind @ 8% of the energy banked.”
sources.source_name: MERC (Distribution Open Access) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2023 (notified 10 November 2023), substituting Reg 20.5/20.4 of DOA Regulations 2016; via MSEDCL Commercial Circular No. 346
sources.caveat: CONFLICT/SUPERSESSION to flag: the original DOA Regulations 2016 (Reg 20.5) set banking charge at 2% in-kind; the Second Amendment 2023 substituted it to 8% in-kind. The current rate is 8%. Unutilised surplus banked energy now lapses at end of each banking cycle (generator gets RECs for lapsed energy).

sources.raw_excerpt: 20.2. The surplus energy from a ‘non-firm’ Renewable Energy Generating Station after set-off shall be banked with the Distribution Licensee.
sources.source_name: MERC (Distribution Open Access) Regulations, 2016, Reg 20 (banking available to non-firm RE OA consumers generally)
sources.caveat: Banking under Reg 20 is available to non-firm RE generating stations supplying OA consumers (both captive and third-party), with no clause restricting banking to captive-only; the 8% in-kind charge applies uniformly. Marked 'estimated' because the regulation does not contain an explicit standalone sentence stating 'third-party banking is allowed' - it is inferred from the general non-firm RE banking entitlement, which is not limited to captive arrangements.

sources.value: 100% exemption for 10 years (long-term captive Green OA projects with >=4hr/50% storage; and captive repowering projects) from CoD
sources.raw_excerpt: ...long-term Captive Green OA projects in Maharashtra and which supply power within Maharashtra which integrate energy storage (for a minimum of 4 hours of 50% of the RE capacity contracted) into the projects ... shall be exempt from payment of Electricity Duty for 10 years from the commissioning date.
sources.source_name: Maharashtra Renewable Energy & Energy Storage Policy 2025-26 to 2035-36, Sections 7.2.2 & 9.4(b)
sources.caveat: Electricity Duty exemption (10 years from CoD) is conditional: it targets long-term Captive Green OA projects integrating storage (>=4hr of 50% of RE capacity) supplying within Maharashtra, and captive repowering projects. Levied under the Maharashtra Electricity Duty Act, 2016. Secondary sources also cite 10yr standalone / 15yr hybrid exemptions but those exact figures were NOT located verbatim in the primary policy text fetched; treat the storage-linked 10-year captive exemption as the verified primary value. Govt retains power to modify duty exemptions (Supreme Court upheld).

sources.caveat: Single-window clearance is provided for Green OA via integrated SLDC/STU/DISCOM portals interfacing with the National Green Open Access Registry.
