research.notes:
PRIMARY SOURCES USED (all official SERC / GoM documents, downloaded and text-extracted via pdftotext):
1. MERC Generic RE Tariff Order FY2025-26, Case No. 1/SM/2025 (8 Apr 2025) - confirms wind tariff is competitive-bidding ONLY, no generic/preferential wind tariff.
2. MERC RE Tariff Regulations 2019 (notified 30 Dec 2019, extended to FY2029-30) - must-run (Reg 13.3), Reg 7.1 competitive bidding for wind.
3. MERC (RPO & REC Framework) First Amendment Regulations 2024 (Maharashtra Gazette, 23 Feb 2024) - Wind RPO + Total RPO trajectory FY2024-25 to FY2029-30.
4. MERC (Distribution Open Access) Regulations 2016 (Reg 3.2, 14, 19, 20) - third-party/captive OA eligibility, banking framework (banking year = FY, original 2% charge).
5. MERC (DOA) Second Amendment Regulations 2023 (Maharashtra Gazette, notified 10 Nov 2023), reproduced verbatim in MSEDCL Commercial Circular No. 346 - 100 kW Green OA threshold, banking charge raised to 8%, CSS/additional-surcharge exemptions, single-window Central Nodal Agency.
6. MERC MSEDCL MYT Order Case No. 217 of 2024 (5th Control Period FY2025-26 to FY2029-30, 827 pp) - approved wheeling charge (Rs 0.62/kVAh HT), wheeling loss 7.5% HT / 12% LT, InSTS transmission loss ~3.28-3.38%, CSS Rs 2.11/kWh (HT-Industry), and the RULING that NO additional surcharge is levied for the 5th Control Period.
7. Maharashtra Renewable Energy & Energy Storage Policy 2025-26 to 2035-36 (Industries/Energy/Labour/Mining Dept, GoM, March 2026, official govt CDN PDF) - repowering incentive (Rs 0.5/kWh 5yr), land allotment, electricity duty exemption (10yr captive+storage), single window, NA-tax waiver. This is the CURRENT policy; it supersedes the Unconventional Energy Generation Policy 2020 for new projects (2015/2020-policy projects remain governed by those policies).

KEY CONFLICTS / SUPERSESSIONS FLAGGED:
- banking_charge: 2% (DOA 2016 original) -> 8% (Second Amendment 2023). Current = 8%.
- banking_period: regulation defines banking YEAR = financial year (annual); secondary sources loosely call it 'monthly' (referring to monthly credit cycle / lapse). Stored as 'annual' per Reg 20.3.
- additional_surcharge: MSEDCL proposed Rs 1.41/unit; MERC REJECTED it for the 5th Control Period (= false now), but it is a charge that can return in future tariff orders - inherently time-bound.
- preferential_tariff: NO value exists (wind is competitive-bidding only) - listed in unresolved because there is no number to record, NOT because a source is missing. This is a confirmed null.

UNRESOLVED (no authoritative VALUE found):
- preferential_tariff: confirmed not to exist as a policy rate (wind = competitive bidding); no Rs/kWh figure can be cited as a 'preferential tariff'.
- green_energy_cess: NO green energy cess exists in Maharashtra. Searched the RE Policy 2025, Circular 346, and MYT Order 217/2024 - none impose a green energy cess (Rs/kWh). Absence of a levy means no value; could not cite a primary clause that names a 'green energy cess' of zero, so listed as unresolved per the accuracy-over-coverage instruction.

CAVEAT ON DEFENSIBILITY: SERC tariff orders and OA charge orders are revised every control period (current MYT control period: FY2025-26 to FY2029-30). All Rs/kWh figures are MSEDCL-specific (Maharashtra's largest discom); Tata Power-D, Adani Electricity, BEST and franchise areas have separately determined charges. Re-verify against the latest MERC order before any legal use.

LOWER-CONFIDENCE / TRANSLATION NOTE: The Unconventional Energy Generation Policy 2020 was available only as a poor Marathi->English machine translation (garbled); no numbers were cited from it. All policy-derived values were taken from the clean official English RE & Storage Policy 2025-26 to 2035-36 instead.

verification.jurisdiction: Maharashtra, India (wind energy policy / open access / RPO / tariff)
verification.overall_confidence_note: Adversarial second-source verification complete for all 24 claimed dimensions. I independently extracted primary text (via pdftotext) from the MERC RPO-REC First Amendment 2024 Gazette, the MSEDCL MYT Order Case 217 of 2024 (827 pp), the MERC DOA Second Amendment 2023 Statement of Reasons, the MERC RE Tariff Regulations 2019, and the Maharashtra RE & Energy Storage Policy 2025-26 to 2035-36, AND cross-checked against independent third-party sources (Mercom India, SaurEnergy, SolarQuarter, Energetica India, EQ Magazine, K&K Advocates, Open Access Exchange, Prayas). 22 of 24 claims CONFIRMED by a second source. 1 REFUTED: banking_period is currently MONTHLY, not annual — the DOA Second Amendment 2023 (effective state of law) changed banking from any annual basis to a monthly cycle where unused banked energy lapses each month (confirmed by K&K Advocates: 'Unused banked energy lapses monthly, clearing uncertainty about whether cycles were annual', and Open Access Exchange: 'restrictive monthly banking framework'). This contradicts the claimed cited basis (DOA 2016 Reg 20.3). 1 UNCERTAIN: additional_surcharge=false is supported unambiguously by the primary MYT Order para 7.36.28 ('not approving the claim to levy of additional surcharge during the 5th Control Period'), but I could NOT find an independent second source specifically confirming this for FY2025-26, and a regulatory tracker (Open Access Exchange) generically states additional surcharge IS applicable per periodic MERC orders — so per adversarial default I mark it uncertain pending a confirming second source. Minor nuances noted in transmission_loss (the 3.38% figure is the FY2024-25 InSTS loss in the order, not an FY2025-26 'commercial loss') and wheeling_charge (figure verified from tariff schedule tables, not the cited Table 437).

verifications.note: Energetica India (independent) confirms MERC 'does not determine a generic tariff for new wind or solar projects' and 'no generic tariff would be specified for new utility-scale solar, wind, biomass, hybrid and co-generation projects' — competitive bidding retained. Also corroborated by Mercom India search result.

verifications.claimed_value: none (no preferential/generic wind tariff determined) Rs/kWh
verifications.note: Same Energetica India source: no generic/preferential tariff is determined for new wind; developers must go through competitive bidding and MERC adopts discovered tariffs. Confirms 'none'.

Open Access Exchange (independent tracker) states Maharashtra supports 'Captive, Group Captive, and Third-Party Open Access models.' DOA Second Amendment 2023 SOR also references procuring power from third party. Confirmed true.

SaurEnergy (independent) confirms 'threshold limit of 100 kW to become eligible for seeking Green Energy Open Access is not applicable to captive consumers' and 'no load limitations apply' to captive consumers. Open Access Exchange confirms captive/group-captive supported. Confirmed true with no load limit.

Threshold reduced from 1000 kW (1 MW) to 100 kW by DOA Second Amendment 2023. Independently confirmed by SolarQuarter, Mercom, SaurEnergy, K&K Advocates, Open Access Exchange, and the RE Policy 2025 (Sec 7.2.1: 'lowering the eligibility threshold for Green Open Access to 100 kW from erstwhile 1 MW'). Primary regulation text (Reg 3.3): 'Consumers having Contract Demand or Sanctioned Load of 100 kW or more...eligible.' Strongly confirmed.

MYT Order Case 217/2024 FY2025-26 tariff schedule (HT categories) shows Wheeling Charge column = 0.62 Rs/unit across HT; para 7.34.16 confirms 'For HT Category, Wheeling charges in Rs/kVAh shall be applicable as approved.' EHV consumers have no wheeling charge (7.34.18), consistent with 'excl EHV.' Open Access Exchange independently states HT wheeling 'typically range between Rs0.60 and Rs0.80 per unit' — 0.62 falls in range. Note: figure verified from FY2025-26 LT&HT tariff summary tables, not specifically the cited 'Table 437.'

verifications.claimed_value: no general wind/RE concession; 10% discount only for Data Centres & semiconductor units
verifications.note: MYT Order para 7.34.19 (verified verbatim from primary): 'the Commission has approved discount of 10% in Wheeling Charges to such Data Centres and semi-conductor units.' No general wind/RE wheeling concession is provided elsewhere in the wheeling section. Confirmed. (Note: independent third-party corroboration for this specific 10% DC/semiconductor concession was thin; verified directly against the primary order text.)

MYT Order Sec 7.35 determines CSS for open access consumers per s.42/86 of Electricity Act 2003. Open Access Exchange independently confirms CSS is applicable (ranges Rs0.80-1.20/unit). Confirmed true.

verifications.claimed_value: no general wind/RE concession; CSS capped at 20% of ACoS; full exemption only for WtE / green-H2 / green-NH3
verifications.note: SaurEnergy (independent) confirms 'Exemptions from cross subsidy surcharge and additional surcharge for power sourced from non-fossil fuel based waste to energy plants and for green energy utilised for the production of green hydrogen and green ammonia.' MYT Order Reg 108.1 (verified) caps CSS at 'not exceeding the 20% of the Average Cost of Supply.' SolarQuarter also confirms WtE/H2/NH3 exemptions. No general wind/RE CSS concession. Confirmed.

Primary MYT Order para 7.36.28 (verified verbatim) unambiguously states: 'the Commission is not approving the claim to levy of additional surcharge during the 5th Control Period' — strongly supports false. HOWEVER I could not find an INDEPENDENT second source that specifically confirms 'no additional surcharge for FY2025-26', and the Open Access Exchange tracker generically states 'Additional Surcharges are also applicable as determined through periodic MERC tariff orders.' Per adversarial default (prefer uncertain over confirmed when no corroborating 2nd source and a generic source contradicts), marked uncertain. The primary order is decisive that it is NOT levied for FY2025-26 to FY2029-30, so the claim is very likely correct for the current period.

verifications.claimed_value: 3.28 % (FY2025-26, intra-state, grossed-up for CSS); InSTS commercial loss 3.38%
verifications.note: MYT Order Table 439 (verified): FY2025-26 Transmission Losses = 3.28% (used in the CSS system-loss 'L' grossing-up). The 3.28% headline is confirmed. Caveat: the 3.38% figure in the order (para 4.3.10) is the InSTS loss the Commission considered for FY2024-25 (truing-up), not an FY2025-26 'commercial loss' — the claim's characterization of 3.38% is slightly off on year/label, but both numbers appear in the primary order. Independent third-party corroboration of these exact loss percentages was not available; verified against primary order.
Mercom India and SaurEnergy independently confirm banking is permitted for green OA consumers in Maharashtra (with 8% charge). DOA Second Amendment 2023 SOR retains banking framework. Confirmed true.

REFUTED for current state of law. K&K Advocates (independent law firm): 'Monthly cycles are confirmed: Unused banked energy lapses monthly, clearing uncertainty about whether cycles were annual.' Open Access Exchange: Maharashtra 'follows a relatively restrictive monthly banking framework' and 'Unused banked energy generally lapses at the end of each monthly settlement cycle.' DOA Second Amendment 2023 SOR (verified primary): banked energy 'offset against consumption in the same time slot during the month' and 'un-utilized surplus banked energy shall be considered as lapsed at the end of each banking cycle' (= monthly). The claimed 'annual' basis citing DOA 2016 Reg 20.3 is superseded by the 2023 amendment; the current banking period is MONTHLY, not annual.

Mercom India (independent): MERC 'increased the banking charges for green energy open access consumers to 8% of the energy banked from 2%.' SaurEnergy and SolarQuarter independently confirm 8%. DOA Second Amendment 2023 SOR (verified primary, substituted Reg 20.4): 'Banking charges shall be adjusted in kind @ 8% of the energy banked.' Confirmed (the 7.5% HT / 12% LT figures in some trackers refer to net-metering banking-loss equivalents for certain HT net-metered consumers, a distinct provision).

DOA Second Amendment 2023 SOR (verified) discusses banking for 'GEOA consumers' generally (not only captive); banking is available to non-firm RE OA consumers broadly. Open Access Exchange confirms banking is permitted across third-party OA models. Confirmed true.

Verified verbatim from primary Gazette text (MERC RPO-REC First Amendment 2024, Reg 7.5(A) table): Wind RPO 2024-25=0.67%, 2025-26=1.45%, 2029-30=3.48%. Independently corroborated by Prayas (Energy Group) comprehensive review of State RPO frameworks citing Maharashtra wind RPO 0.67%/1.45%. Wind RPO defined (Reg 2.1(n)(a)) as met by WPPs commissioned after 31 Mar 2024. Confirmed.

Verified verbatim from primary Gazette text (Reg 7.5(A) table): Total RPO 2024-25=29.91%, 2025-26=33.01%, 2029-30=43.33% (full series: 29.91/33.01/35.95/38.81/41.36/43.33). Corroborated by Prayas comprehensive State RPO review (Maharashtra total 29.91%/33.01%). Confirmed.

MERC RE Tariff Regulations 2019 Reg 13.3 (verified verbatim from primary): 'all RE Power Projects shall be treated as Must Run Projects and shall not be subjected to merit order despatch principles.' SolarQuarter independently confirms must-run status under Reg 13.3. Confirmed true.

verifications.claimed_value: Rs 0.5/kWh for first 5 years (competitive bidding to MSEDCL) + 10-year Electricity Duty exemption (captive repowering)
verifications.note: RE Policy 2025 Sec 9.4 (verified verbatim): (a) projects selling to MSEDCL via competitive bidding eligible for 'additional Government of Maharashtra incentive payment of Rs 0.5/kWh for the first 5 years from CoD'; (b) captive repowering 'eligible for Electricity Duty exemption for the first 10 years from CoD.' Mercom India and SaurEnergy independently confirm Rs 0.5/kWh for 5 years (1 GW repowering target by 2030) plus 10-yr ED exemption for captive repowering. Confirmed.

verifications.claimed_value: Govt land tiered (GP <=15 acres, PS 15-25, ZP >25); private lease = higher of 6% of land value or Rs 1,25,000/hectare/yr, +3% annual escalation; NA tax/premium waived
verifications.note: RE Policy 2025 Secs 6.1-6.3 (verified verbatim): local-body land up to 15 acres at Gram Panchayat level, 15-25 acres at Panchayat Samiti, >25 acres at Zilla Parishad (Sec 6.1); private lease base = 'higher of: 6% of the land value...or Rs 1,25,000 per hectare...increase by 3% annually' (Sec 6.2); 'Non-agricultural tax/premium shall be waived' (Sec 6.3). EQ Magazine independently confirms 'higher of 6% of land value or Rs1.25 lakh/hectare/year, with 3% annual escalation' and govt land at Rs1/year for 30 years. Confirmed.

verifications.claimed_value: false (no separate GP NOC mandated; for local-body land up to 15 acres, allotment is made AT Gram Panchayat level)
verifications.note: RE Policy 2025 Sec 6.1 (verified verbatim): 'If the land required for the project is up to 15 acres, it may be made available at the Gram Panchayat level.' The Gram Panchayat is the ALLOTTING authority for sub-15-acre local-body land; no separate GP NOC is independently mandated by the policy. The claim's interpretation matches the primary text. (No independent third-party article addresses this specific NOC nuance; verified against the primary policy text, which is itself authoritative.)

verifications.claimed_value: 100% exemption for 10 years (long-term captive Green OA with >=4hr/50% storage; and captive repowering) from CoD
verifications.note: RE Policy 2025 Sec 7.2.2 (verified verbatim): 'long-term Captive Green OA projects...which integrate energy storage (for a minimum of 4 hours of 50% of the RE capacity contracted)...shall be exempt from payment of Electricity Duty for 10 years from the commissioning date.' Plus Sec 9.4(b) captive repowering 10-yr ED exemption. Mercom India independently confirms the 10-year ED exemption for captive green OA with 50% capacity / 4-hour storage. Confirmed.

RE Policy 2025 Sec 7.2.1 (verified verbatim): the process 'must ensure single window clearance of all required aspects of seeking Green OA for all eligible consumers in a time-bound manner.' MEDA has developed a single-window web portal for RE projects (Sec referenced). EQ Magazine independently confirms 'single window clearance for Open Access applications.' Confirmed true.

sources.raw_excerpt: the generic rate approved by KSERC for wind energy projects without the benefit of accelerated depreciation ... CV Renewables ultimately accepted the Rs 3.94/unit generic tariff

sources.caveat: Rs 3.94/kWh is the generic wind tariff under the (now-superseded) RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020, confirmed via a secondary report of the KSERC interim order dated 20-Mar-2025 (primary order PDF not located). Under the newer RE & Related Matters Regulations 2025 (notified 5-Nov-2025) wind FiTs are metering-specific: net metering Rs 2.87/kWh (Reg 28), net billing Rs 4.15/kWh (Reg 29.5), gross metering Rs 4.20/kWh (Reg 30.5). sources.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 33 – Captive RE consumer); also RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020 (Chapter IV)
sources.caveat: Captive use is permitted in both frameworks; captive RE consumers are exempt from cross-subsidy surcharge but pay transmission/wheeling charges, losses and grid support charges. 2025 regs currently stayed by Kerala HC; 2020-regs captive provisions independently in force.

sources.raw_excerpt: 20.1(ii) Open Access shall be granted as per the provisions under KSERC (Connectivity and Intra State Open Access) Regulations, 2013 to consumers, who require a supply of electricity where the maximum power to be made available at any time exceeds 100 kW
sources.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 20.1(ii))
sources.caveat: Open access for RE is granted to consumers whose maximum power exceeds 100 kW, aligned with the MoP Green Energy Open Access Rules 2022 threshold. The 2020 regs used a 1 MW threshold for the open-access/IPP chapter (Reg 22); the 100 kW threshold is the newer 2025 position but is currently STAYED by Kerala HC (10-Nov-2025). General (non-RE) intra-state OA threshold continues under the KSERC Connectivity & Intra-State Open Access Regulations, 2013 (1 MW).

sources.raw_excerpt: 34.2 A consumer purchasing power from an IPP supplying power to a third party by availing open access shall pay to the licensee the following charges approved by the Commission from time to time: (i) Transmission charges; (ii) Wheeling charges; (iii) Cross subsidy surcharges; (iv) Transmission losses and Distribution losses
sources.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 34.2); RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020 (Reg 30)
sources.caveat: CSS applies to third-party-sale (IPP) open-access RE consumers. Express CSS EXEMPTIONS exist for: captive consumers (Reg 33.2 proviso), virtual-net-metering/self-wheeling prosumers (Reg 32 / 2020 Reg 17(6)). The numeric CSS rate itself is set in the KSERC retail-supply/OA tariff orders, not the RE regulations. 2025 regs currently stayed by Kerala HC.

sources.raw_excerpt: The balance units, if any, will be carried forward to next billing period, after deducting a monthly banking charge of 2% of the balance units. This banking facility shall be allowed till the end of each financial year
sources.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 28/29 banking provisions); RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020 (Regs 20-21)
sources.caveat: Banking is permitted for prosumers/captive consumers. 2025 regs currently stayed by Kerala HC; 2020 regs (banking with 5% charge per settlement period) is the last unstayed banking framework.

sources.raw_excerpt: The balance units, if any, will be carried forward to next billing period, after deducting a monthly banking charge of 2% of the balance units. This banking facility shall be allowed till the end of each financial year; ... The balance units remaining, if any, in the credit of the prosumer at the end of the financial year shall be monetized at the applicable APPC/ FiT
sources.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 28(vi)-(viii))
sources.caveat: Under 2025 regs, banked energy carries forward (with a 2% monthly deduction) until the end of each financial year, then unused credit is monetized at APPC/FiT (annual settlement). Under the 2020 regs the 'settlement period' for non-solar (wind) sources is annual: 1 April to 31 March (Reg 2(1)(bk)(ii)). Both frameworks = annual settlement. 2025 regs currently stayed by Kerala HC.

sources.raw_excerpt: The balance units, if any, will be carried forward to next billing period, after deducting a monthly banking charge of 2% of the balance units.
sources.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 28(vi))
sources.caveat: CONFLICT BETWEEN REGIMES: 2025 regs = 2% monthly banking charge on balance units (Reg 28(vi)). Prior RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020 = 5% of energy banked at end of billing period, non-cumulative (Reg 21(4)(i): '5% of the banked energy shall be accounted towards banking charges'). The 2025 regs (2% monthly) are STAYED by Kerala HC (10-Nov-2025); the 2020 figure (5% per settlement) is the last unstayed rate. Treat as contested until the HC matter resolves (next hearing 1-Dec-2025).

sources.raw_excerpt: 'Banking facility' means such facility whereby the prosumer or the captive generator of Renewable Energy System injects ... and to be carried forward to the subsequent billing periods of the settlement period
sources.source_name: KSERC RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020 (Reg 2 definition + Regs 20-21); RE & Related Matters Regulations 2025 (Reg 28 banking)
sources.caveat: Banking is defined for the prosumer/captive generator's own surplus carried forward against own consumption; the regulations do not provide a third-party (merchant/IPP-to-multiple-consumer) banking facility — banking is tied to self-consumption/net adjustment. Marked false on that basis, but the regs are not fully explicit on prohibiting third-party banking, so confidence is estimated. (KSEBL does enter inter-state inter-utility banking agreements, a distinct mechanism.)

sources.raw_excerpt: Financial Year ... HPO WPO DRE Other RPO Total RPO ESO | 2025 – 26  2.40  1.75  3.00  34.85  42.00  0
sources.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 41.1, Table 8)
sources.caveat: Wind Power Obligation (WPO) FY2025-26 = 1.75%, rising to 1.80 (FY26-27), 1.90, 2.00, 2.20 (FY29-30). Wind RPO ('WPO') applies only to energy from wind projects commissioned after 31-Mar-2022 (per MoP order). The 2024 Second Amendment to the 2020 regs had identical FY2025-26 WPO=1.75 (Total=43). The 2025 regs Table 8 is the latest but is STAYED by Kerala HC; the 2024 amendment trajectory is the last unstayed RPO schedule.

sources.source_name: KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 (Reg 41.1, Table 8)
sources.caveat: Total RPO FY2025-26 = 42.00% (HPO 2.40 + WPO 1.75 + DRE 3.00 + Other RPO 34.85), rising to 50% by FY2029-30. CONFLICT: the 2024 Second Amendment (to the 2020 regs) set FY2024-25 Total=40% and FY2025-26 Total=43% (no DRE column). The 2025 regs (Total=42%) are STAYED by Kerala HC; the 2024 amendment figures (40% FY24-25 / 43% FY25-26) are the last unstayed values. Reconcile after HC resolution.

sources.raw_excerpt: All the renewable energy power plants, unless and otherwise exempted, shall be treated as 'MUST RUN' power plants and shall not be subjected to 'Merit order Dispatch' principles.
sources.source_name: KSERC RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020 (Reg 38(1) – Principles for dispatch)
sources.caveat: Must-run status is express in the 2020 regs (Reg 38). The 2025 regs do not restate the phrase 'must run' but defer scheduling/dispatch to the KSERC (Forecasting, Scheduling, Deviation Settlement) Regulations for solar & wind; RE must-run status is also mandated nationally (Electricity Act / IEGC 2023). Treat as true and durable across both frameworks.

research.notes:
LEGAL STATUS / KEY CAVEAT (read first): Kerala currently has two competing regulatory frameworks plus a stay. (1) KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025 — notified No. 3228/Con.Engg/2023/KSERC dated 5-Nov-2025, control period FY2025-26 to FY2029-30 — is the LATEST notified RE regulation and the primary source for most dimensions above. BUT the Kerala High Court (Justice Mohammad Niaz C P) STAYED its 'operation, implementation, enforcement, and all further proceedings' by order dated 10-Nov-2025 (challenge by the Domestic On-Grid Solar Power Prosumers Forum; net-metering cap to 3 kW and export charges among the grounds); matter posted to 1-Dec-2025. Because of the stay, the prior (2) KSERC (Renewable Energy and Net Metering) Regulations, 2020 (notified 07-Feb-2020) as amended by the 2nd Amendment 2024 (RPO; No. 230/Con.Engg/2023/KSERC dated 7-Aug-2024) is the last UNSTAYED framework. I have cited the 2025 regs as primary where they are the operative text and flagged every conflict with the 2020/2024 regime in the per-dimension caveats. A legally-defensible tool should track the HC outcome before relying on any 2025-specific value (especially banking_charge 2% vs 5%, geoa_threshold 100 kW vs 1 MW, rpo_total 42% vs 43%).

PRIMARY DOCS USED: 2025 RE & Related Matters Regs (https://dev.erckerala.org/api/storage/draft-regulations/WHGPRV4PWntRfaPDsgbf2asrVyAPoW7dJLaVrK1p.pdf — note: served from the 'draft-regulations' path but the file content is the FINAL notification dated 5-Nov-2025; gazette version also at https://dev.erckerala.org/api/storage/final-regulations/BDm5OZBLIuAbHoyTX0xoploDz82DbFy1O6klxxfS.pdf); 2020 RE & Net Metering Regs (https://jameskutty.info/rules/KSERCrenregn20.pdf); 2024 2nd Amendment / Statement of Reasons (https://dev.erckerala.org/api/storage/final-regulations/LmZUspGQkDFXH09A6VCIqebXJwqHZfQHJ313nSNZ.pdf); Draft Kerala Power Policy 2025 (https://anert.gov.in/assets/ckeditor/ckfinder/uploads/files/policy/policy%203/Kerala%20-%20Power%20Policy%202024%20-v13_2024-12-18_WM.pdf). HC stay reported by Onmanorama/The Week/Down To Earth (10-11 Nov 2025). Wind generic tariff Rs 3.94 via WindInsider report of KSERC Mar-2025 interim order.

UNRESOLVED DIMENSIONS — WHY: wheeling_charge, wheeling_concession, css_concession, additional_surcharge and transmission_loss are NOT fixed in the RE regulations; both the 2020 (Reg 30) and 2025 (Regs 20.1(iv), 34.2) regs state these are 'as approved by the Commission from time to time' / per the KSERC (Connectivity & Intra-State Open Access) Regulations 2013 and the periodic retail-supply/OA tariff orders. I could not locate the specific numeric Kerala OA/wheeling tariff order PDF (Schedule of Open Access Charges, effective 01-Oct/01-Nov-2024) to extract defensible numbers, so I left these unresolved rather than guess. NEXT STEP to resolve: obtain the KSERC retail-supply tariff order / Schedule of Open Access Charges for KSEBL (FY2024-25 onward) from erckerala.org — it carries the per-kWh wheeling charge, the wheeling concession (if any) for RE, the CSS schedule by consumer category and any RE CSS concession, additional surcharge (if levied), and the approved intra-state transmission + distribution loss percentages. green_energy_cess: no green energy cess is found in any KSERC regulation or in the (draft) Kerala Power Policy; a Rs 0.05/unit figure appears only in a secondary law-firm compilation (claruslaw.com) without a traceable primary GO, so it is left unresolved (likely no notified green energy cess in Kerala). Partial/estimated dimensions (repowering_incentive, land_allotment, gram_panchayat_noc, single_window, electricity_duty_exemption) rest on the DRAFT Kerala Power Policy 2025 only ('Draft for Discussion. NOT FOR EXTERNAL CIRCULATION') and are NOT legally enforceable until the final policy is gazetted; electricity_duty_exemption in particular is scoped to green hydrogen/ammonia, not general wind. Kerala also has separate (not retrieved here) Kerala Solar Policy and Kerala Small Hydro Policy referenced by the draft — Kerala has historically had no standalone notified Wind Policy, with wind governed mainly through the KSERC RE regulations + tariff orders.

verifications.note: Two independent sources corroborate. Mercom (independent) reports the Ramakkalmedu generic wind tariff as Rs 3.93/kWh WITH accelerated depreciation (Rs 4.23/kWh without AD). The claimed WindInsider source and Omnicore both confirm KSERC's interim order (March 20, 2025, CV Renewables 250 kW Ramakkalmedu) approved a Rs 3.94/unit ceiling tariff aligning with the KSERC generic wind tariff under the RE & Net Metering Regulations 2020. CAVEAT: 3.94 is a project-specific PPA ceiling matching the generic (AD) wind tariff, NOT a special 'preferential' feed-in incentive scheme; labelling it 'preferential_tariff' is a slight mischaracterization, and figures Rs 3.93/4.10/4.23 also circulate for the same wind zone depending on AD and vintage.

Primary text confirmed verbatim: Reg 45.1 of the notified KSERC (RE & Related Matters) Regulations 2025 (No. 3228/Con.Engg/2023/KSERC, 5-Nov-2025) states 'Tariff Based Competitive Bidding (TBCB) under Section 63 of the Act shall be the preferred mode for power procurement by the Distribution licensees for all RE sources (other than DRE) and ESS.' Independent second source (Renewable Watch) confirms 'procurement... must primarily follow tariff-based competitive bidding.' CAVEAT: TBCB is the PREFERRED, not exclusive, mode; generic/project-specific tariff (Sec 62) remains available with justification. Also: these 2025 Regulations were STAYED by the Kerala High Court on 10-Nov-2025 pending challenge, so their legal force was suspended as of the analysis date.

Confirmed in two primary instruments. 2025 Regs Reg 34 (Independent Renewable Power Generator) Reg 34.2 expressly contemplates 'an IPP supplying power to a third party by availing open access.' 2020 Regs Reg 22(1)(iii) defines 'Renewable energy system installed by a Renewable Energy Generator as an Independent power producer, for third party sale using the transmission and/or distribution system of the utility.' Third-party sale via open access is permitted. CAVEAT re 2025 source: stayed by Kerala HC 10-Nov-2025; the 2020 Regs remain the operative instrument and independently establish this.

Confirmed in two primary instruments. 2025 Regs Reg 33 ('Captive RE consumer') fully establishes captive RE generation/consumption per Rule 3 of Electricity Rules 2005, with wheeling and 33.2 proviso that 'no cross-subsidy surcharge shall be applicable for the Captive consumer.' 2020 Regs Chapter IV and Reg 5 (RPO of captive consumer) independently establish captive use. CAVEAT: 2025 Regs stayed by Kerala HC 10-Nov-2025; 2020 Regs remain operative and independently support captive_use=true.

Verified verbatim in the primary notified text: Reg 20.1(ii) states 'Open Access shall be granted as per the provisions under KSERC (Connectivity and Intra State Open Access) Regulations, 2013 to consumers, who require a supply of electricity where the maximum power to be made available at any time exceeds 100 kW.' Could NOT locate a fully independent second source quoting this exact 100 kW open-access trigger (press coverage focused on solar/prosumer net-metering limits of 3 kW / 500 kW / 3 MW). Confidence rests on the primary document only. CAVEAT: 2025 Regs stayed by Kerala HC 10-Nov-2025.

Confirmed for THIRD-PARTY open access in the primary text: 2025 Regs Reg 34.2(iii) lists 'Cross subsidy surcharges' among charges payable by a consumer purchasing from an IPP supplying a third party via open access. 2020 Regs likewise impose CSS on third-party open access. IMPORTANT NUANCE that the claim's boolean flattens: CSS does NOT apply to captive consumers (2025 Reg 33.2 proviso: 'no cross-subsidy surcharge shall be applicable for the Captive consumer'). So css_applicable=true is correct only for third-party/IPP open access, not captive. CAVEAT: 2025 Regs stayed by Kerala HC 10-Nov-2025.

Confirmed in two primary instruments. 2025 Regs Reg 28(vi) (prosumers) and Regs 33.3/34.3 (captive/IPP) establish banking of surplus energy. 2020 Regs Reg 2(e) defines 'Banking facility' and Regs 20-21 provide banking/settlement. However note the claimed citation 'Reg 28/29' for banking in the 2025 Regs is the Net Metering / Net Billing accounting sections (banking lives inside Reg 28 NMS, not a standalone banking regulation). CAVEAT: 2025 Regs stayed by Kerala HC 10-Nov-2025; 2020 Regs remain operative and independently support banking_allowed=true.

Verified in primary text: 2025 Regs Reg 28(vi) 'This banking facility shall be allowed till the end of each financial year' and 28(viii) 'balance units remaining... at the end of the financial year shall be monetized... paid... before 30th day of April of the subsequent financial year.' Settlement is annual (financial-year). Could not find an independent second source stating the period explicitly. NUANCE: 'annual' holds for prosumer/captive banking; for third-party IPP open access (Reg 34.3/34.4) banking is within-timezone and surplus LAPSES (no annual carry/payment). CAVEAT: 2025 Regs stayed by Kerala HC 10-Nov-2025.

Verified verbatim in primary text (multiple clauses: prosumer Reg 28(vi), captive Reg 33.3, etc.): '...carried forward to next billing period, after deducting a monthly banking charge of 2% of the balance units.' Matches the claim exactly. Could not find an independent second source quoting the 2% monthly figure. IMPORTANT CONTRAST: the prior/operative 2020 Regs set a 5% (per billing period, non-cumulative) banking charge, NOT 2% monthly. So 2% monthly is correct ONLY under the 2025 Regs, which were STAYED by the Kerala HC on 10-Nov-2025; if the stay holds, the operative banking charge reverts to the 2020 Regs' 5%.

Supported by two primary instruments. 2020 Regs Reg 2(e) restricts the 'Banking facility' to 'the prosumer or the captive generator' (not third-party-sale IPPs). 2025 Regs independently corroborate: for IPP open-access third-party supply, Reg 34.3(v)/34.4(iii) provide that surplus energy 'shall lapse and the distribution licensee is not under any obligation to make payments' (the IPP only gets RECs) - i.e., no banking carry-forward/settlement for third-party transactions. So banking_third_party=false is well supported.

Verified verbatim in primary text: Reg 41.1 Table 8, row FY2025-26 shows WPO (Wind Power Obligation) = 1.75%. Exact match to claim. Could NOT find an independent second source quoting 1.75% (regulatory-tracker/press coverage confirmed the existence of RPO+ESO and a 5-yr control period but did not publish the per-source percentages). Confidence rests on the primary notified document only. CAVEAT: 2025 Regs stayed by Kerala HC 10-Nov-2025, so these RPO targets' legal effect was suspended pending the challenge (next hearing 1-Dec-2025).

Verified verbatim in primary text: Reg 41.1 Table 8, FY2025-26: HPO 2.40 + WPO 1.75 + DRE 3.00 + Other RPO 34.85 = Total RPO 42.00%, with ESO = 0 for FY2025-26 (ESO rises 1.00->4.00% across the control period). NUANCE in the claim label: the claim says 'Total RPO incl. ESO', but for FY2025-26 ESO=0 so 42.00% is Total RPO and ESO is a separate additional obligation (Reg 41.2 lets stored RE count toward total RPO). Could not find an independent second source quoting 42.00%. CAVEAT: 2025 Regs stayed by Kerala HC 10-Nov-2025.

Verified verbatim in the cited primary source (2020 Regs, which are the operative instrument): Reg 38(1) 'All the renewable energy power plants, unless and otherwise exempted, shall be treated as MUST RUN power plants and shall not be subjected to Merit order Dispatch principles.' Exact match to claim. This aligns with CERC/national must-run treatment of RE; high confidence and not dependent on the stayed 2025 Regs.

verification.overall_confidence_note: CRITICAL CROSS-CUTTING CAVEAT FOR A LEGALLY-DEFENSIBLE TOOL: (1) Every claim sourced to the 'KSERC (Renewable Energy and Related Matters) Regulations, 2025' (tariff_mode, third_party_sale, captive_use, geoa_threshold_kw, css_applicable, banking_allowed, banking_period, banking_charge, rpo_wind, rpo_total) was textually CONFIRMED against the genuine notified PDF (No. 3228/Con.Engg/2023/KSERC dated 5-Nov-2025) - the document at the claimed URL is real and its provisions match the citations. HOWEVER, the Kerala High Court STAYED these 2025 Regulations on 10-Nov-2025 (confirmed independently by Onmanorama press and King Stubb & Kasiva legal tracker) for procedural/consultation defects, with the matter listed for 1-Dec-2025. As of analysis (corpus context), their legal force was suspended; if the stay holds, the operative instrument reverts to the KSERC (RE & Net Metering) Regulations 2020 (where, e.g., the banking charge is 5%/billing-period, not 2%/month). A defensible tool must surface this stay. (2) SECOND-SOURCE LIMITATION: independent corroboration was strong for the wind tariff (Mercom vs WindInsider/Omnicore), must_run (2020 Regs), third_party_sale/captive_use/banking (dual 2020+2025 primary instruments), and the structural existence of RPO/TBCB/net-metering (Renewable Watch, Energetica, Urja Daily, SolarQuarter). But the EXACT NUMERIC figures - WPO 1.75%, Total RPO 42.00%, 2% monthly banking charge, 100 kW open-access threshold - were verifiable ONLY in the primary notified text; no fully independent source published those precise numbers, so those four are 'confirmed against primary' rather than 'confirmed by a distinct second source.' (3) All five Power Policy claims (repowering_incentive, land_allotment, gram_panchayat_noc, single_window, electricity_duty_exemption) were verified against the primary ANERT-hosted document, but that document is an internal DRAFT ('Draft for Discussion. NOT FOR EXTERNAL CIRCULATION', Dec-2024 v13) - provisions are proposed and non-binding until a final policy is notified; no independent second source was found. (4) Two labeling nuances worth flagging downstream: css_applicable=true holds for third-party/IPP open access but NOT captive (2025 Reg 33.2 proviso exempts captive from CSS); and 'preferential_tariff' is really a generic/PPA-ceiling wind tariff (Rs 3.93 with AD / 4.23 without), not a distinct preferential FiT incentive.

sources.raw_excerpt: Selection of projects under InSTS category (excluding open access projects) shall be through a competitive bidding process, as per the requirement of APDISCOMs to fulfil RPO target fixed by the APERC or any requirements beyond RPO target subject to the approval of the APERC. Project developer shall be allowed to develop the MW scale wind power projects through open access route under this Policy for sale of energy within the State subject to evacuation feasibility.
sources.source_name: Andhra Pradesh Integrated Clean Energy Policy, 2024 (G.O.Ms.No.37, Energy (Power-II) Dept, dt.30.10.2024), clause 7.1.1
sources.caveat: AP has moved away from preferential/feed-in wind tariffs. New wind PPAs with DISCOMs are via competitive bidding (SECI/NTPC for ISTS; DISCOM bids for InSTS). No state-fixed preferential wind tariff exists under the 2024 policy. Policy valid 5 years from 30.10.2024.

sources.value: none (competitive-bidding determined; no APERC-fixed preferential wind tariff)
sources.raw_excerpt: Selection of projects under InSTS category (excluding open access projects) shall be through a competitive bidding process... Project developer shall be allowed to develop the MW scale wind power projects through open access route under this Policy for sale of energy within the State subject to evacuation feasibility.
sources.source_name: Andhra Pradesh Integrated Clean Energy Policy, 2024 (G.O.Ms.No.37), clause 7.1.1
sources.caveat: No numeric preferential ₹/kWh tariff is fixed for wind. Tariffs are discovered through competitive bidding and approved by APERC. Listed under unresolved because no single defensible ₹/kWh value exists.

sources.raw_excerpt: The Clean Energy Projects not fulfilling the criteria of Captive generating plant shall be considered as third-party sale projects and Cross-subsidy surcharge and Additional surcharge shall be levied as determined by APERC from time to time for consumption from third party Clean Energy Projects.
sources.source_name: Andhra Pradesh Integrated Clean Energy Policy, 2024 (G.O.Ms.No.37), clause 5.8(a)
sources.caveat: Third-party sale is permitted but attracts CSS and additional surcharge (no concession). Governed by APERC GEOA Regulation No. 3 of 2024.

sources.raw_excerpt: All registered companies, Government entities, partnership companies/firms, individuals, and all consumers of APDISCOM(s) will be eligible for setting up of Clean Energy Projects within the State for sale of electricity/captive use, in accordance with the Electricity Act 2003.
sources.source_name: Andhra Pradesh Integrated Clean Energy Policy, 2024 (G.O.Ms.No.37), clause 5.2
sources.caveat: Captive use is allowed; captive plants (per Electricity Rules) are exempt from CSS and additional surcharge and have no GEOA load limit (clauses 5.8, 5.10).

sources.raw_excerpt: Open access shall be eligible for all consumers with contracted demand or sanctioned load of 100 kW or more through single connection or multiple connections aggregating to a 100 kW or more located in the same division of a DISCOM, except for captive consumers who shall not have any load limitation.
sources.source_name: Andhra Pradesh Integrated Clean Energy Policy, 2024 (G.O.Ms.No.37), clause 5.10(b); confirmed in APERC GEOA Regulation No. 3 of 2024, clause 2.1(f)
sources.caveat: 100 kW threshold for green energy open access; no load limit for captive consumers. Per APERC GEOA Regulation No. 3 of 2024 (effective 01.05.2024).

sources.raw_excerpt: Wheeling Charges for 5th Control Period. Voltage / Financial Year 2024-25 ... 33 kV (Rs./KWH) 0.32 ... 11 kV (Rs./KWH) 0.62 ... LT (Rs./KWH) 1.09
sources.source_name: APERC Multi-Year Order on Determination of Wheeling Tariffs of DISCOMs' network for 5th Control Period FY2024-25 to FY2028-29 (Press Note dt.27.09.2024)
sources.caveat: Wheeling charge is voltage-level specific (per-kWh basis, first time on per-unit basis). Rises each year (e.g. 33kV ₹0.38 in FY2025-26). Wheeling charges waived if injection and withdrawal are at the same voltage level (policy clause 5.7). Order covers FY2024-25 to FY2028-29.

sources.value: 50 (only for domestic, public utilities and government institutions; no concession for C&I/industrial GEOA)
sources.raw_excerpt: These charges, which are now uniform and similar to the retail supply tariffs, come with a fifty per cent concession for domestic consumers, public utilities, and government institutions.
sources.source_name: APERC Multi-Year Wheeling Tariff Order, 5th Control Period (Press Note dt.27.09.2024), Important Directions (i)
sources.caveat: The 50% concession applies only to domestic/public-utility/government consumers, NOT to commercial/industrial GEOA users. GEOA RE consumers otherwise pay full wheeling charges (GEOA Reg 3/2024 clause 12 grants no concession).

Hence, the Commission is not inclined to grant any exemption or concession in this regard... Cross subsidy Surcharge as applicable, and determined by the Commission in Retail Supply Tariff Order... shall be paid by the GEOA consumers.
sources.source_name: APERC (Green Energy Open Access, Charges, and Banking) Regulation No. 3 of 2024, clause 12(c) and clause 13
sources.caveat: CSS IS applicable to GEOA/third-party consumers. NOT leviable for captive consumers, waste-to-energy supply, or green hydrogen/ammonia production. CSS value itself is set in the periodic Retail Supply Tariff Order, not fixed in this regulation.

The Commission is not inclined to grant any exemption or concession to GEOA on these aspects so as to balance the interests of the consumers at large and hence the draft is retained.
sources.source_name: APERC (Green Energy Open Access, Charges, and Banking) Regulation No. 3 of 2024, clause 13 (Commission's Analysis and Decision)
sources.caveat: Stakeholders requested 50% CSS / waiver; APERC explicitly declined. Full CSS applies (0% concession) except full exemption for captive, WtE, and green hydrogen/ammonia. RE Manufacturing projects get 10-year CSS exemption (policy clause 5.8(b)).

sources.raw_excerpt: The Cross Subsidy Surcharge, and Additional Surcharge shall be determined as per the provisions of the Electricity Act, 2003 and the National Tariff Policy... Provided that cross subsidy surcharge and additional surcharge shall not be leviable in case open access is provided to a person who has established a captive generating plant... sources.raw_excerpt: APPROVED: TRANSMISSION LOSS TRAJECTORY AND SYSTEM AVAILABILITY ... 2024-25 99.70% 2.75 2.95% 2.55% ... 2025-26 99.70% 2.75 ... 2026-27 99.70% 2.70
sources.source_name: APERC Transmission Tariff Order for 5th Control Period FY2024-25 to FY2028-29, Table No. 3.2

This is the InSTS transmission loss only; DISTRIBUTION/WHEELING losses are separate, voltage-pair and DISCOM specific (e.g. APSPDCL LT-to-LT ~4.13%, 33kV-to-33kV ~2.99% in FY2024-25 per the Wheeling Tariff Order).

sources.raw_excerpt: Banking: No generator other than the Wind, Solar and Mini Hydel power generators shall be allowed the facility of banking which shall be subject to the following conditions.

sources.caveat: Banking allowed specifically for Wind, Solar and Mini-Hydel generators. Effective 01.05.2024; subject to first amendment (Regulation 3 of 2024 amendment process ongoing 2025).

sources.raw_excerpt: The Banking shall be on a monthly billing cycle basis. Each calendar month shall be treated as one billing cycle. The banked energy shall be utilized within the same billing cycle failing which the unutilized energy at the end of the billing cycle shall lapse, and no compensation whatsoever shall be claimed/paid for such lapsed banked energy.
sources.source_name: APERC (Green Energy Open Access, Charges, and Banking) Regulation No. 3 of 2024, clause 14(1)
sources.caveat: Monthly billing-cycle banking; unutilised energy lapses at month-end with no compensation. Permitted banked quantum capped at 30% of consumer's total monthly DISCOM consumption.

sources.value: 8 (in kind); 12 in kind if drawn during peak ToD hours
sources.raw_excerpt: The Banking charges in kind shall be 8% of the energy banked at the consumer end. ... the drawal of banked energy during the peak load hours as mentioned in the ToD approved by the Commission in the Retail Supply Tariff Orders shall be charged 12 per cent in kind of banked energy.

sources.caveat: Banking charge is 8% in kind of energy banked; rises to 12% in kind for drawal during peak ToD hours. Banked quantum limited to 30% of monthly DISCOM consumption. Drawal not permitted in peak hours during R&C measures.

sources.raw_excerpt: The permitted quantum of banked energy by the Green Energy Open Access consumers shall not be more than thirty percent of the total monthly consumption of electricity from the distribution licensee by the consumers.

sources.caveat: Banking is available to GEOA consumers generally (both captive and third-party open-access wind/solar/mini-hydel), not restricted to captive. The regulation applies banking conditions to 'Green Energy Open Access consumers' without excluding third-party transactions.

research.notes:
PRIMARY SOURCES USED (all official APERC/GoAP PDFs, HTTP 200 verified):
1. AP Integrated Clean Energy Policy 2024 — G.O.Ms.No.37, Energy (Power-II) Dept, dt.30.10.2024 (valid 5 years). https://aperc.gov.in/admin/upload/G.O.Ms.No.37Dated30.10.2024.pdf — the operative state RE/Wind policy GR. Supersedes AP Wind Power Policy 2018 (G.O.Ms.No.2 dt.03.01.2019).
2. APERC (Green Energy Open Access, Charges, and Banking) Regulation No. 3 of 2024, dt.01.05.2024. https://aperc.gov.in/admin/upload/Orderdated01052024GEOAREGULATION.pdf — source for GEOA threshold, CSS/additional surcharge, banking.
3. APERC Multi-Year Wheeling Tariff Order, 5th Control Period FY2024-25 to FY2028-29 (Press Note dt.27.09.2024). https://aperc.gov.in/admin/upload/PressNoteonMultiYearOrderontheDeterminationofWheelingTariffsofDISCOMSsnetworkforthe5thControlperiodFY202425toFY202829.pdf — source for per-kWh wheeling charges + 50% domestic/govt concession + voltage-pair distribution losses.
4. APERC Transmission Tariff Order, 5th Control Period FY2024-25 to FY2028-29. https://aperc.gov.in/admin/upload/TransmissionTarifforderfortheperiodFY202425toFY202829.pdf — source for transmission loss (2.75% avg FY2024-25).
5. RPO: APERC RPPO/REC Regulation No. 5 of 2022 (Total RPO 20/22/24% for FY24-25/25-26/26-27), as cited in the policy GR Section 3.

KEY LEGAL CAVEATS / CONFLICTS:
- TARIFF MODE: AP wind is now competitive-bidding-only; there is NO state-fixed preferential ₹/kWh wind tariff under the 2024 policy (left unresolved as a number; tariff_mode captured as competitive_bidding). APPC is not used.
- RPO CONFLICT: The policy GR reproduces TWO trajectories — (a) Ministry of Power GoI (Total 29.91-35.95%, with explicit wind sub-targets 2.46-4.29%) and (b) APERC's own binding Reg No. 5 of 2022 (Total 20-24%, single combined target, no separate wind sub-obligation). rpo_total uses APERC's binding figure; rpo_wind uses the GoI wind trajectory (flagged: not an APERC-adopted wind-only obligation).
- CHARGES ARE TARIFF-ORDER-DEPENDENT and revised periodically: wheeling charges (5th control period, FY24-25 to FY28-29) and CSS (set in annual Retail Supply Tariff Orders, not in the GEOA regulation). transmission_loss trajectory revises to 2.70% from FY2026-27.
- LOSSES: There is no single transmission/wheeling loss number. Intra-state TRANSMISSION loss = 2.75% (FY24-25). DISTRIBUTION/WHEELING losses are voltage-pair and DISCOM specific (range ~3-11%; e.g. APSPDCL LT-to-LT ~4.13%, 33kV-to-33kV ~2.99% FY24-25).
- WHEELING CONCESSION (50%) applies ONLY to domestic/public-utility/government consumers, NOT industrial/commercial GEOA — captured with that caveat.
- ELECTRICITY DUTY: 2024 policy gives NO blanket duty exemption for wind generation (only 10-yr reimbursement for RE Manufacturing). Differs from the older 2018 Wind Policy.

UNRESOLVED:
- green_energy_cess: No per-kWh green energy cess exists in AP. The policy levies 'Green Energy Development Charges' (GEDC) but as a per-MW/year fee (Nil for DISCOM procurement; INR 1 Lakh/MW/yr for 12 yrs from COD, then 1.5, then 2 Lakhs/MW/yr — fees table clause 17.11), NOT a ₹/kWh cess on energy. No authoritative ₹/kWh cess figure exists, so left unresolved.
- gram_panchayat_noc: The ICE Policy 2024 does not address any gram-panchayat NOC requirement for wind projects. It exempts AP Pollution Control Board NOC (clause 5.14) and provides single-window clearance, but is silent on local-body/panchayat NOC. No authoritative primary source pins this requirement either way for AP, so left unresolved.

Also corroborated by JMK Handbook copy and APERC Order O.P.91/2024 (EQ Mag) which notes DISCOMs select wind via competitive bidding instead of generic tariffs. Confirmed.

Resource-allocation clause (k) allows SERC Section-62 tariff only for the state's right-of-first-refusal up to 30% of allotted capacity; the general route is tariff-based competitive bidding. No fixed preferential wind tariff found. Confirmed.

APERC GEOA Regulation No.3 of 2024 (primary, verified locally) clause 7: 'all entities shall be eligible to take power through Green Energy Open Access for third-party sale or captive use'. ICE Policy clause 5.8(a) (official G.O.) treats non-captive Clean Energy Projects as 'third-party sale projects'. Triple-confirmed. APERC GEOA Reg No.3/2024 clause 2.1(f) verbatim: 'Entity means any consumer who has contracted demand or sanctioned load of hundred kW or more...except for captive consumers' (no load limit for captive). ICE Policy 5.10(b) repeats '100 kW or more'. Independent second source Mercom India quotes the same 100 kW definition; Renewable Watch also confirms. Confirmed.

But the '12% in kind for peak-ToD drawal' is REFUTED: that 12% figure existed only in the DRAFT clause 14(4); the FINAL ADOPTED regulation removed it - final clause 14(4) sets peak/off-peak DRAWAL rules with NO 12% charge (no '12' or 'twelve per cent' appears anywhere in the adopted order). verification.overall_confidence_note: High overall confidence. I obtained and locally text-extracted the primary documents (APERC GEOA Regulation No.3/2024, APERC Wheeling Tariff Press Note 27.09.2024, APERC Transmission Tariff Order 5th CP, and the ICE Policy G.O.Ms.No.37 from BOTH the APERC-hosted copy and the official government NREDCAP server, plus the JMK Handbook copy), and corroborated each value with at least one independent reputable second source (The Hindu, Mercom India, Renewable Watch, NSEFI, Economic Times, Power Line). 19 of 21 claims are fully confirmed by two or more sources with verbatim clause text. TWO items need correction: (1) banking_charge - the 8% base is correct but the '12% peak-ToD' component was in the DRAFT only and was REMOVED from the adopted regulation (verdict: refuted); recommend dropping the 12% figure. (2) banking_third_party - value 'true' is correct, but the cited clause 14(3) is wrong (it is the 30% quantum cap); the correct citation is GEOA reg clause 14 chapeau + clause 7. Minor citation nuances also noted for third_party_sale (5.8(a) addresses CSS treatment of third-party sales rather than enabling them; enabling is GEOA clause 7) and css_concession (AP grants 0% concession per clause 13; a separate MoP GEOA Rules 2022 statutory ceiling of 50% exists but is not an AP concession).

research.notes:
PRIMARY SOURCES USED: (1) MPERC RE Tariff Regulations 2024 {G-43(I) of 2024}, notified 02/08/2024, in force to 31 Mar 2027 — fetched full text. (2) MPERC Retail Supply Tariff Order FY 2025-26 (Petition 72/2024, 29/03/2025) — fetched full text; source of wheeling Rs 0.19/kWh@33kV, additional surcharge Rs 1.24/kWh, transmission loss 2.61%, voltage-wise losses, CSS methodology (WAPP incl. RPO Rs 3.66/unit). (3) MPERC Open Access Regs 2021 (Rev-I) 5th Amendment {ARG-24(I)(v) of 2025}, gazette 07/03/2025 — fetched (this 'fifth amendment' is to OPEN ACCESS regs, NOT RPO; it bars short-term non-green OA for RPO-defaulting entities). (4) CBIP consolidated summary of MPERC Cogeneration/RE Generation Regulations — fetched (older 2010-rev consolidation; framework for wheeling/CSS/additional-surcharge/banking/third-party/captive). (5) Prayas (Energy Group) primary comment doc quoting BOTH RPO trajectory tables. 

KEY STRUCTURAL FINDINGS: 
- MP has NO generic preferential wind tariff: wind <10 MW = project-specific (Sec 62), wind >=10 MW = competitive bidding (Sec 63). preferential_tariff marked UNRESOLVED (no rupee figure exists in any order). 
- tariff_mode = project_specific for small wind; competitive_bidding for large wind. 
- Open-access charges (wheeling/CSS/additional surcharge/losses) are NOT in the RE policy or RE-generation regs — they are set ANNUALLY in the retail supply tariff order; values given are FY 2025-26 and will change each year. 

CONFLICTS FLAGGED: 
- RPO: earlier-notified MPERC trajectory (Wind 3.36% / Total 30.97% for FY25-26) vs MoP-aligned Fifth Amendment to Cogeneration Revision-II Regs (Wind 1.45% / Total 33.01% for FY25-26; 29.91% FY24-25 → 43.33% FY29-30), reported notified ~April 2026. Captured the MoP-aligned figures as latest but flagged both. 
- Banking: older Cogeneration regs = ANNUAL (FY) cycle, unadjusted energy purchased at lowest bid rate; current GEOA Methodology Regs 2023 = MONTHLY (calendar-month) cycle, 8% banking charge, no carry-forward. DISCOM-registered captive projects get NO banking. 
- wheeling_charge: primary order Rs 0.19/kWh@33kV vs RESCO secondary Rs 0.18 — primary used. 

FETCH LIMITATIONS: invest.mp.gov.in primary RE Policy 2025 PDF returned HTTP 403 (and >10 MB via Exa); RE-Policy values quoted from finraja/r-solar/Free Press Journal/Energetica/Down To Earth reproductions of the policy text (cross-corroborated, hence 'extracted' where text was verbatim-quoted by those secondaries). Several live mperc.in PDFs (GEOA Methodology Regs 2023, Petition 42/2024) timed out on the fetch tool — banking 8%/monthly and 100 kW GEOA threshold therefore marked 'estimated' (sourced from Mercom + the retail order's cross-reference). 

RECOMMENDED PRIMARY-DOC VERIFICATION BEFORE LEGAL USE: (a) latest gazette-notified MPERC Cogeneration Revision-II Regs 2021 incl. Fifth Amendment for the operative RPO table + Reg 11.2(d) CSS-captive text; (b) MPERC GEOA Methodology (Open Access & Banking Charges) Regs 2023 + amendments for verbatim 8% banking charge, monthly cycle, 100 kW threshold; (c) the full 45-page invest.mp.gov.in RE Policy 2025 PDF for the verbatim wheeling-waiver/duty/land/single-window/repowering clauses and to confirm absence of any Gram Panchayat NOC requirement.
